findings.rationale: The upstream property is a generic URL, while ours specifically applies to RepairProvider entities.
findings.qaRationale: Upstream 'url' is an undefined generic catch-all property with no domain, so it carries no semantic anchor identifying a repair provider; matching our domain-specific property to it is name-level similarity, not concept identity.

findings.rationale: Our property is a specific URL for a RepairProvider, whereas schema.org’s serviceUrl is a generic website to access any service.
findings.qaRationale: Both are URL-valued properties with overlapping intent, but ours identifies a repair provider entity's web presence (domain RepairProvider) while schema:serviceUrl denotes the access endpoint of a service channel, so they overlap without a clean subsumption.

findings.rationale: The upstream term refers to a certification agency’s URL, which is a more general concept than the repair provider’s specific URL.
findings.qaRationale: A repair provider's URL and a certification-agency's URL denote unrelated roles, so despite both being organisation-URL properties they are not the same concept.

findings.rationale: Our property specifies a URL for a repair provider, which is a more specific use of the generic website URL defined upstream.
findings.qaRationale: Both are URL-valued properties with overlapping intent, but ours denotes a repair provider's web presence while gs1:referencedFileURL is a generic file/resource reference, so they overlap without a clean subsumption.

findings.rationale: Our property specifies disassembly instructions, a subset of general safe use instructions.
findings.qaRationale: Safe disassembly/end-of-life handling instructions are a different concept from generic safe-use instructions, and the upstream term's lack of definition plus the use-vs-disassembly divergence does not support a genuine subset relation.

findings.rationale: Both properties record the date of the most recent update, though one refers to a DPP instance and the other to a web page.
findings.qaRationale: schema:lastReviewed records when content was checked for accuracy/completeness, not when it was changed; the true update analogue is schema:dateModified, so review and update are distinct concepts.

findings.rationale: Our property records the last update of any DPP, whereas the upstream term refers only to updates of a live blog.
findings.qaRationale: schema:liveBlogUpdate links a LiveBlog to its update postings, an unrelated domain and meaning to a DPP's last-modified timestamp, so they are not the same concept.

findings.rationale: Both refer to the European Community EC number, but the candidate term is framed for a component rather than a substance.
findings.qaRationale: Both encode the EINECS/ELINCS EC number identifier, but ours attaches it to a SubstanceOfConcern while the undefined upstream term frames it for a component, so the property meaning overlaps without being interchangeable.

findings.rationale: Both represent impact values for lifecycle modules, but the candidate lacks a specific definition.
findings.qaRationale: Ours is the value of one impact indicator for a single EN 15804 lifecycle module, whereas the candidate (label 'ImpactValues', plural, undefined) denotes a collection/container of impact values rather than a single-module result, so they are not the same concept.

findings.rationale: Our property specifies distribution‑stage emissions, a subset of the generic carbon footprint concept.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties yielding a carbon-footprint quantity, and distribution-stage emissions are a stage-scoped specialization of the generic product carbon footprint, so ours is narrower—though the relation is partly partitive (one stage of a multi-stage declaration) which weakens confidence.

findings.rationale: Both properties refer to an organization that validates or acknowledges a credential, but one is specifically the verifier while the other denotes recognition of validity.
findings.qaRationale: recognizedBy denotes an organization that acknowledges a credential's validity (domain: credential), which is distinct from the body that actively performed third-party verification of a due-diligence report, so the concepts and domains do not align.

findings.rationale: Both properties represent the human‑readable name of a material or component within a composition.
findings.qaRationale: Names align exactly and both are component-name properties, but the upstream term carries no definition to confirm identical scope, so a weaker grade than EXACT is warranted.

findings.rationale: Our property refers to any material or component name, whereas the upstream property is limited to ingredient or ingredient group names.
findings.qaRationale: Both are free-text name properties for a product's constituents, but ingredientName is scoped to ingredients/additives (food-style) while componentName covers materials/fibres/alloys, so they overlap without one cleanly subsuming the other.

findings.rationale: The candidate refers specifically to additive or genetic modification names, which is a narrower subset of the broader component/material name concept.
findings.qaRationale: gs1:additiveName names a food additive or genetic modification present/absent in a product, a distinct concept from the name of a constituent material/fibre/alloy in a material-composition breakdown, so they are not the same concept despite superficial name overlap.

findings.rationale: The property specifies the name of a material component, which is a more specific instance of the generic item name defined by schema.org.
findings.qaRationale: Both are human-readable naming properties, but schema:name labels the subject resource itself whereas oec:componentName labels the constituent material/component related to a MaterialComposition, so it is an overlapping rather than a clean strict specialization.

findings.rationale: Both terms represent the same functional unit concept for carbon footprint metrics.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties denoting the functional/declared unit for a product's carbon footprint declaration, and our term is explicitly minted as a 1:1 alias of UNTP declaredUnit with matching name and type.

findings.rationale: Our property specifies functional units for carbon‑footprint metrics, whereas the upstream property is a general unit of measurement code.
findings.qaRationale: declaredUnit is an LCA/EPD reference (functional) unit to which impact metrics are normalized (e.g. kg CO2e/kWh), a different concept from schema:unitCode's generic UN/CEFACT measurement-unit code for a quantitative value, so they are not the same concept and not cleanly subsumed.

findings.rationale: declaredUnit specifies a functional unit for carbon metrics (e.g., kg CO2e/kWh), which is a more specific concept than the generic Measurement Unit defined by UNECE.
findings.qaRationale: declaredUnit is an LCA/EPD functional-reference unit (often a composite expression like kg CO2e/kWh that is not a valid UN/ECE Rec 20 code), so it is a different concept from gs1:unitCode's enumerated measurement-unit code rather than a specialization of it.

findings.rationale: The candidate refers to a specific CO₂ emissions unit (g/km), whereas our property covers any functional unit for carbon metrics.
findings.qaRationale: Our property denotes the functional reference unit (denominator basis) for declaring carbon metrics, whereas schema:emissionsCO2 is a concrete CO2 emission quantity value in g/km — different concepts, not a broader/narrower version of the same one.

findings.rationale: Our Value property accepts any JSON data type, whereas schema.org’s value is limited to values of QuantitativeValue, PropertyValue, etc., making the upstream term a narrower specialization.
findings.qaRationale: Both are generic property-value holders for a value node, but ours is scoped to EN 18223 SingleValuedDataElement/MultiLanguageValue while schema.org's is scoped to QuantitativeValue/PropertyValue nodes, so they overlap without one cleanly subsuming the other.

findings.rationale: The generic value property encompasses any JSON data type, whereas codeValue is a specific textual code used to identify a value.
findings.qaRationale: schema:codeValue is a short identifying code for a coded term, whereas oec:value is a generic holder for any data element value, so they are different concepts rather than a broader/narrower pair.

findings.rationale: Our property represents any value of a data element, whereas the candidate is limited to certification standard values.
findings.qaRationale: oec:value is a generic structural value-container for an EN 18223 data element, while gs1:certificationValue is the domain-specific standard value of a certification; the apparent link is only lexical name overlap, not a shared concept.

findings.rationale: Both properties denote a generic value, but the upstream term is specifically tied to evidence support, whereas ours applies broadly to any data element.
findings.qaRationale: Upstream m8g:value is a CCCEV-specific relation binding an Evidence to the value it supports for an Information Concept, whereas ours is a generic data-element value container from EN 18223; they share only the name, not the concept.

findings.rationale: Our Value property accepts any JSON data type, whereas GS1’s Measurement Value is limited to a numeric value qualified by a unit code.
findings.qaRationale: Despite the shared name, gs1:value is the numeric slot of a measurement quantity pattern (paired with gs1:unitCode), while oec:value is the generic value slot of an EN 18223 data element accepting any JSON type; they belong to different structural patterns and are not the same concept.

findings.rationale: Our term encompasses all consumer incentives such as refunds, credits, and replacements, whereas schema.org cashBack refers specifically to a cardholder benefit that pays a percentage of expenditures.
findings.qaRationale: schema.org cashBack is a credit-card cardholder reward feature, an entirely different concept from a product take-back/recycling incentive, sharing only the word 'back'; they are not the same concept.

findings.rationale: Our property describes any consumer incentive (refund, credit, replacement), whereas the upstream property is limited to return fees for customer remorse.
findings.qaRationale: An end-of-life take-back recycling incentive (refund/credit/replacement for returning a product) is a different concept from a retail customer-remorse return fee, not a broader version of it.

findings.rationale: Our property covers all consumer incentives, while schema.org’s incentiveType is limited to tax‑related incentive categories.
findings.qaRationale: schema.org/incentiveType categorizes fiscal incentives (tax credits, subsidies) in a financial-incentive context, whereas our property is a free-text description of consumer take-back rewards (refund/credit/replacement) in an end-of-life program, so they are not the same concept despite sharing the word 'incentive'.

findings.rationale: Our class represents a full type‑III EPD, while the candidate denotes an individual block within such a declaration.
findings.qaRationale: Both classes concern the EPD concept, but 'EPDBlock' (no definition) most plausibly names the passport's EPD container/section rather than a sub-block of a declaration, so the first-pass BROAD reading is unsupported and an overlapping CLOSE match is the defensible grade.

findings.rationale: Our property specifies use‑phase emissions with a QuantitativeValue range, whereas the candidate is a generic carbon footprint property.
findings.qaRationale: Both are carbon footprint properties, but ours is scoped to the use-phase lifecycle stage while the untp candidate is a generic carbon footprint, so they overlap without one cleanly subsuming the other.

findings.rationale: Our class represents a specific language‑tagged string, which is a particular type of structured value; thus it is narrower than the general StructuredValue.
findings.qaRationale: schema:StructuredValue is a generic superclass for composite values decomposed into multiple typed parts (quantities, monetary amounts, coordinates), whereas a language-tagged string is a primitive string-plus-language-tag that schema.org models via @language/inLanguage rather than as a StructuredValue, so they are not the same concept.

findings.rationale: Our property specifies a URL for a carbon footprint study, which is a more specific use of the generic website URL defined upstream.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties carrying a URL to a referenced document, and ours is a semantically constrained subtype (specifically the carbon footprint study) of the generic gs1:referencedFileURL, so ours is narrower.

findings.rationale: The property specifies the issuer of a Digital Product Passport, which is a more specific instance of the generic organization issuing an item defined upstream.
findings.qaRationale: Both properties point to an issuing organization, but ours adds ongoing maintenance responsibility and targets a DPP via a distinct OperatorInformation range, so the concepts overlap rather than ours being a clean subset of schema:issuedBy.

findings.rationale: Our property specifies production‑stage emissions with a QuantitativeValue range, whereas the candidate is a generic carbon footprint property.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties carrying a carbon-footprint value, but ours is restricted to one lifecycle stage (production) whereas the untp term is a generic, whole-product carbon footprint, making ours a narrower specialization.

findings.rationale: Our property specifies the URI of a compostability certification standard, which is a more specific instance of the generic certification standard defined upstream.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties naming the standard a claim is certified against, but ours is restricted to compostability certifications while gs1:certificationStandard covers any certification standard, making ours a narrower instance of the same concept.

findings.rationale: Both properties provide safe use instructions for a substance of concern, matching in purpose and expected data type.
findings.qaRationale: Identical property name and plausibly the same concept, but the upstream term carries no definition or domain, so interchangeability cannot be confirmed and EXACT overstates the match.

findings.rationale: The candidate property covers general consumer usage instructions for a product, while our term specifically addresses safe use instructions for the substance of concern.
findings.qaRationale: Both are free-text instruction properties with genuine overlap, but ours is scoped to substance-of-concern safety handling (REACH-style) rather than general product usage, so it is not a clean subset of consumer usage instructions—CLOSE, not NARROW.

findings.rationale: Both describe precautions related to handling a product, but our term focuses on safe use of the substance itself while the candidate is specific to preparation or consumption.
findings.qaRationale: Our term covers chemical safety instructions tied to a substance of concern, whereas the GS1 candidate is narrowly about food preparation/consumption precautions, so they are distinct concepts despite both being 'precautions'.

findings.rationale: Our property provides specific safe‑use instructions for a substance of concern, which is a narrower instance of the broader consumer safety information defined upstream.
findings.qaRationale: Safe-use instructions for a substance of concern are a specific subtype of the broader consumer safety information, so our property is narrower than the upstream one.

findings.rationale: Our class covers all material composition information, while the upstream class is limited to textile materials.
findings.qaRationale: Both are composition classes that overlap, but ours centers on source-country/percentage/critical-raw-material framing for any material while the GS1 class is textile-fiber-composition specific, so neither cleanly subsumes the other rather than ours being a strict superset.

findings.rationale: Our property specifies units for environmental impact indicators, a more specific use case than the generic measurement unit defined upstream.
findings.qaRationale: Our property's domain is restricted to environmental impact indicator results (LCIA units like kg CO2 eq, mol H+ eq) whereas gs1:unitCode is a UN/ECE Rec 20 trade measurement unit code; the conceptual scopes and code-list bases differ enough that they are not the same property.

findings.rationale: Both properties express a unit of measurement for a numeric value, but the impact indicator unit is broader than a dose unit.
findings.qaRationale: schema:doseUnit denotes the unit of a medication dose, an unrelated pharmaceutical concept, whereas oec:indicatorUnit denotes the unit of an LCA environmental impact indicator; sharing the generic notion of 'unit' is name-level overlap, not the same concept.

findings.rationale: Both properties denote a unit of measurement for a numeric value, but the impact indicator unit is broader than a drug unit.
findings.qaRationale: Both name a measurement unit but apply to wholly unrelated domains—environmental LCA impact indicators versus pharmaceutical drug dosage—so they are not the same concept.

findings.rationale: Both properties refer to a URL that identifies an external entity providing verification or certification, but the upstream term is specifically for issuing agencies while ours covers any third‑party assurance.
findings.qaRationale: Ours is the URL of an independent third-party verification/assurance of a due-diligence report, whereas the GS1 term is the URL of the agency that issues a certification standard, so they denote different entities and concepts.

findings.rationale: Our property applies to all remanufactured products, while the upstream term is specific to rail item reconditioning.
findings.qaRationale: Both are date properties for a product-restoration event, but reconditioning, refurbishment, and remanufacturing are distinct (not cleanly nested) restoration tiers and the upstream term carries no definition, so an overlapping CLOSE match is safer than asserting strict subsumption.

findings.rationale: Both classes represent the same concept of supply‑chain traceability metrics.
findings.qaRationale: Both are classes for supply-chain traceability metrics and the names align, but the upstream UNTP class carries no definition to confirm true 1:1 interchangeability, so the weaker grade is warranted.

findings.rationale: TraceabilityPerformance is a specific type of performance metric, whereas PerformanceMetric is a general class.
findings.qaRationale: Our class is one specific performance-metric category (proportion of materials verifiably traced), making it a subtype of UNTP's generic PerformanceMetric class, so ours is narrower.

findings.rationale: The SCIP ID is a specific type of identifier, whereas schema.org’s identifier property covers all kinds of identifiers.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties and a SCIP database identifier is a specific instance of schema.org's generic identifier concept, so ours is genuinely narrower than the catch-all target.

findings.rationale: Operator Role is a specific type of role within the supply chain, which falls under the broader category of OrganizationRole.
findings.qaRationale: schema.org OrganizationRole is a reified role held within an organization (membership-style, time-bounded), whereas oec:OperatorRole denotes an economic operator's function in the supply chain/market, not a role inside an organization, so it is not a subclass of the candidate.

findings.rationale: Both terms denote the role of an economic operator within a supply chain.
findings.qaRationale: Both are classes for an economic operator's role, but the upstream term lacks any definition to confirm true interchangeability, so a weaker grade is warranted.

findings.rationale: Our class describes a specific piece of a multi‑piece trade item, whereas IndividualProduct is a general single product instance.
findings.qaRationale: GS1 ITIP (AI 8026) identifies one piece within a multi-piece trade item sharing a single GTIN, which is a component-piece concept, not schema.org's serialized unique product instance, so neither is a kind of the other.

findings.rationale: Both classes represent a single physical piece of a multi‑piece trade item identified by GS1 AI 8026.
findings.qaRationale: The upstream is the GS1 AI 8026 (ITIP) application-identifier data element, an encoding/identification scheme, while our term is an ontology class for the physical piece itself; they share the same ITIP domain but an identifier scheme is not interchangeable with the concept it identifies.

findings.rationale: Both properties represent the same circularity metric for materials.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties named for the well-known Ellen MacArthur MCI metric, but the upstream term carries no definition to confirm identical scope (same 0-1 recycled-input/recyclability/utility composite), so interchangeability cannot be asserted and the weaker grade is warranted.

findings.rationale: LifecycleStage is a specific enumeration of life‑cycle modules, whereas StatusEnumeration is a generic list of status types.
findings.qaRationale: EN 15804 lifecycle modules are phases of a product's life cycle, not status types, so LifecycleStage is not a kind of StatusEnumeration despite both being enumerations.

findings.rationale: Both properties denote the proportion of materials that are verifiably traced through the supply chain.
findings.qaRationale: Identical property name and plausibly the same traceability-performance metric, but the upstream term carries no definition to confirm true interchangeability, so a weaker overlapping grade is warranted.

findings.rationale: Passport Status is a specific lifecycle token for a DPP, while Certification Status broadly indicates active/inactive certification status.
findings.qaRationale: DPP lifecycle status and certification active/inactive status describe entirely different subjects (a passport versus a certification), so despite both being status properties they are not the same concept.

findings.rationale: Our property refers specifically to the lifecycle status of a Digital Product Passport, whereas schema.org’s certificationStatus applies generally to any certification.
findings.qaRationale: A DPP lifecycle status is not a kind of certification status; the subjects (a product passport vs a certification) are different concepts, so name-level 'status' similarity does not make ours a narrowing of schema:certificationStatus.

findings.rationale: Both properties represent the same concept of circularity performance data for a product.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties carrying a product's circularity performance data structure; identical IRI label and matching UNTP semantics make them interchangeable despite the upstream definition being blank.

findings.rationale: Both properties refer to metrics of product circularity, but the candidate term is a generic indicator while our term denotes overall performance data.
findings.qaRationale: Our property is a container for overall circularity performance data, whereas the candidate is one specific circularity metric (the Material Circularity Indicator), so ours subsumes it rather than being interchangeable.

findings.rationale: Both properties provide repair or maintenance instructions, but the GS1 term focuses on a link to service info while ours is broader repairability information.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties conveying repair/maintenance information, but GS1 serviceInfo is a simple hyperlink to service instructions while ours references a structured RepairabilityInfo object, so they overlap without being interchangeable or cleanly nested.

findings.rationale: Both properties express a duration in years, but our term specifically refers to spare parts availability while the candidate is a general warranty period.
findings.qaRationale: Spare-parts availability duration and warranty-promise duration are distinct concepts that merely share a duration datatype, so they are not the same property.

findings.rationale: Both properties denote the mass fraction of a material expressed as a decimal between 0 and 1.
findings.qaRationale: Both are material-composition mass-fraction properties with identical naming, but the upstream term carries no definition to confirm the 0-1 decimal scale and exact semantics, so a strict reading warrants closeMatch rather than asserting interchangeability.

findings.rationale: Single-Valued Data Element represents a single point value, whereas QuantitativeValue also allows intervals; thus our class is a narrower specialization.
findings.qaRationale: SingleValuedDataElement is a structural/cardinality classification of any data element (string, code, date, or number) from EN 18223's data-model meta-layer, whereas QuantitativeValue specifically models a numeric quantity or interval with a unit, so they are different concepts rather than one being a narrowing of the other.

findings.rationale: Our class specifies a single point value, whereas the GS1 Measurement Type also allows intervals; thus our concept is a narrower specialization.
findings.qaRationale: Our class is a data-modeling cardinality construct (a data element holding exactly one value of any datatype, per EN 18223), whereas gs1:QuantitativeValue is a unit-bearing measurement value; the point-vs-interval overlap is superficial and they are not the same concept.

findings.rationale: The term describes any single data point, whereas the candidate refers specifically to an item within a data feed.
findings.qaRationale: Our term is an EN 18223 metamodel primitive denoting a data element with single-value cardinality, whereas schema.org DataFeedItem is a syndication entry within a DataFeed; the apparent overlap is mere name similarity, not shared meaning.

findings.rationale: Single-Valued Data Element specifies a single point value, whereas PropertyValueSpecification is a general specification that can include single or multiple values; our concept is narrower.
findings.qaRationale: oec:SingleValuedDataElement is a structural data-element type from EN 18223's data-model taxonomy, whereas schema.org PropertyValueSpecification describes input constraints for a web action/form parameter, so they are different concepts despite a superficial cardinality parallel.

findings.rationale: The upstream property represents recycled content in general, while our term specifies the pre‑consumer (post‑industrial) fraction.
findings.qaRationale: Both are recycled-content properties, but ours measures only the pre-consumer (post-industrial) fraction, a strict subset of the upstream's overall recycled content, so our term is narrower.

findings.rationale: Both properties represent the fraction of pre‑consumer recycled content, with identical scope and range.
findings.qaRationale: The identically-named property almost certainly denotes pre-consumer recycled content fraction, but with no upstream definition, range, or domain given, interchangeability cannot be confirmed, so EXACT is unwarranted.

findings.rationale: Both properties identify a material or component within a composition, using the same string-based identifier.
findings.qaRationale: The names and property type align and the concept plausibly matches, but the upstream term has no definition to confirm interchangeability, so EXACT is unwarranted and CLOSE is the stricter defensible grade.

findings.rationale: The candidate property is a general identifier for any Thing, while our term specifies an identifier specifically for material components.
findings.qaRationale: Both are identifier properties and ours is a domain-restricted specialization of schema:identifier's generic any-Thing identifier, so ours is the narrower concept.

findings.rationale: Both properties link an earlier instance to a newer one, but the schema.org property is generic for product variants while our term is specific to passport version history.
findings.qaRationale: Our term links a passport to its prior passport version (a metadata-versioning relation between digital records), whereas schema:predecessorOf links one physical product variant to its successor, so the concepts only partially align and ours is the narrower, document-versioning specialization.

findings.rationale: Both properties link to a prior version of the same object, though our term is specific to passports while the upstream term refers generally to assets.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties asserting a link to the immediately prior version of the same entity, but adms:prev is domain-scoped to ADMS Assets (semantic assets) rather than passports, so the predecessor-version semantics overlap without being fully interchangeable.

findings.rationale: Both classes represent the same concept of a product passport containing identification, lifecycle metadata, and product data.
findings.qaRationale: Both are top-level product-passport envelope classes covering the same concept, but ours is bound to the EN 18223 information model while UNTP's is independently governed with its own structure, so they overlap strongly without being strictly interchangeable.

findings.rationale: The Digital Product Passport is a specific type of electronic file, thus it is narrower than the generic schema.org DigitalDocument.
findings.qaRationale: A DPP is a structured product-data envelope/information model, not an electronic file or office-style document; schema.org DigitalDocument (whose subtypes are notes, spreadsheets, text, presentations) is a different concept and the match rests only on the word 'Digital'.

findings.rationale: Both properties describe a product’s category, but the GS1 property is specific to GPC codes while ours refers to ESPR classification.
findings.qaRationale: Both are product-classification descriptors, but ours names a value typed to an ESPR ProductCategory class while the GS1 property is specifically the textual description of a GPC category code, so ours is the broader/different-scheme concept rather than interchangeable — and strictly it should be NONE since ours points to a class instance and GS1's is a literal description, but they overlap enough that ours-narrower-than-a-generic-category-descriptor does not hold; treating GPC as one specific classification scheme, ESPR category is a different scheme, making this CLOSE at most.

findings.rationale: Our property specifies the jurisdiction for an EPR registration, a specific type of legislation, whereas the candidate refers to any legislative jurisdiction.
findings.qaRationale: schema:legislationJurisdiction denotes the jurisdiction a piece of legislation originates from (domain Legislation), whereas oec:eprJurisdiction denotes the member state where a producer's EPR registration applies (domain ExtendedProducerResponsibility); the shared word 'jurisdiction' masks distinct subjects and an origin-vs-applicability semantic mismatch.

findings.rationale: EPR Jurisdiction refers to a country or member state, whereas addressRegion denotes a sub‑national region within a country.
findings.qaRationale: Ours is a property whose value is a country (gs1:Country) identifying the EPR registration's member state, whereas schema:addressRegion denotes a sub-national first-level division within a country, so they identify different administrative levels and are not the same concept.

findings.rationale: Our property refers specifically to the jurisdiction of an EPR registration, a narrower concept than the general legal jurisdiction described by the candidate.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties expressing a jurisdiction relation, and our term is a domain-constrained specialization (the country/member-state of an EPR registration) of schema.org's general legal-jurisdiction property, justifying a broadMatch/NARROW grade.

findings.rationale: Both properties represent the same concept of a decimal fraction indicating how much of a product can be recycled.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties expressing a recyclable fraction of a product, but the upstream term has no published definition to confirm interchangeability and 'recyclable content' (proportion of recyclable material present) is not provably identical to a 'recyclability rate', so a weaker overlap grade is warranted.

findings.rationale: Both properties quantify the recyclable portion of a product, but the candidate term lacks the explicit decimal fraction definition and domain specification.
findings.qaRationale: Both quantify a recycling-related fraction of a product, but recyclabilityRate measures the portion that *can be* recycled (end-of-life recyclability) whereas untp:recycledContent measures the portion *already made from* recycled material (input content), so they are related but not the same concept.

findings.rationale: The upstream class represents a general evaluation condition, whereas our class specifies a particular laboratory method for biodegradability assessment.
findings.qaRationale: A standardised laboratory test method is a procedure, not a condition/requirement for evaluation, so it is not a subtype of CCCEV Criterion despite both relating to assessment.

findings.rationale: Our property specifies the aggregated total value of an indicator, whereas the candidate term denotes a generic indicator; thus our concept is a narrower specialization.
findings.qaRationale: The undefined upstream 'indicator' property most plausibly links to an indicator concept/entity, whereas ours is a decimal aggregate magnitude of an indicator's value, so they denote different concepts rather than a broader/narrower pair.

findings.rationale: Indicator Total Value is a specific numeric measurement qualified by unit, whereas Measurement Value is a generic numeric value with unit; our term is a narrower specialization.
findings.qaRationale: Both are unit-qualified decimal measurement values, but oec:indicatorTotalValue is domain-bound to an aggregate lifecycle-impact indicator result rather than a logically narrower sub-concept of GS1's generic measurement value, so they overlap without one strictly subsuming the other.

findings.rationale: Our property is a specific numeric aggregate value of an impact indicator, whereas schema.org’s ‘value’ is a generic property for any quantitative or structured value.
findings.qaRationale: schema.org/value is a generic untyped value slot on QuantitativeValue/PropertyValue nodes, not a domain-specific aggregate impact-indicator total, so they are not the same concept and the alias would be semantically meaningless.

findings.rationale: The GS1 ‘Instructions’ property is a general link to any instructions, whereas our property specifically refers to repair instructions and manuals.
findings.qaRationale: Both are link-type properties; GS1 'instructions' is a generic link to any instructions (assembly, usage, etc.) of which repair instructions is one specific subtype, so our term is properly narrower.

findings.rationale: The GS1 ‘Service information’ link covers all service or maintenance instructions, while our property specifically targets repair instructions and manuals.
findings.qaRationale: GS1 serviceInfo links broadly to service or maintenance instructions, and repair instructions are a narrower subset of that documentation scope, so ours is the more specific concept.

findings.rationale: Both properties capture the name of the entity that operates or manages an Environmental Product Declaration program.
findings.qaRationale: The upstream term 'epM' has an opaque label and no definition, providing no verifiable evidence it denotes an EPD programme operator name, so no graded match can be responsibly asserted.

findings.rationale: Our class represents a generic ordered list of identical data elements, while the candidate is a specific set of colour code details; thus our term is broader.
findings.qaRationale: Ours is a generic structural meta-concept (an ordered list of same-typed data elements), while gs1:ColourCodeDetails is a specific product colour-code attribute set; they are unrelated concepts, not a broader/narrower pair.

findings.rationale: Both properties provide a URL to a product‑specific information page, though the specific context (EPREL vs SmartLabel) differs.
findings.qaRationale: EPREL URL points specifically to the EU's mandatory energy-label registry entry while gs1:smartLabel links to GS1's voluntary consumer-disclosure portal; sharing the generic shape of 'a product info URL' is name/shape similarity, not the same concept.

findings.rationale: Both properties represent the systematic IUPAC chemical name of a substance.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties carrying the systematic IUPAC chemical name of a substance with identical meaning and string range, differing only in domain scope which does not affect property semantics.

findings.rationale: The candidate property refers to an IUPAC name of a component within a product, whereas our term specifies the IUPAC name of an individual hazardous substance.
findings.qaRationale: Both properties carry the systematic IUPAC name of a chemical entity and their string values are interchangeable, but ours is scoped to a hazardous substance while the candidate targets a generic product component, so they overlap without being identical.

findings.rationale: Both properties represent the same concept of emissions performance data, with identical labels and aligned definitions.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties carrying an emissions-performance data container and OUR term explicitly mirrors UNTP's emissionsPerformance 1:1 (same role, same range class), making them interchangeable despite the upstream lacking an inline definition.

findings.rationale: Our property is a general emissions performance container, whereas the candidate refers to a specific CO₂ emission value.
findings.qaRationale: Ours is a structured emissions-performance container (range oec:EmissionsPerformance, aligned with UNTP), while schema:emissionsCO2 is a single vehicle-specific CO₂ scalar in g/km — different concepts, not a broader/narrower form of the same one.

findings.rationale: Both properties denote the date after which an item (EPD or generic object) is no longer valid.
findings.qaRationale: Both denote an end-of-validity date, but ours is a domain-restricted specialization scoped to EPD documents whereas schema:validUntil is a generic validity-end date for offers/items, so ours is narrower rather than interchangeable.

findings.rationale: Both properties denote the end date of a validity period, though the candidate is used for general items while ours is specific to environmental product declarations.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties expressing a validity end date, but ours is a domain-restricted specialization (EPD validity) of schema.org's generic validThrough, making our concept narrower.

findings.rationale: Our property specifies a single end date for EPD validity, whereas the upstream term denotes a general validity period that may include both start and end dates.
findings.qaRationale: Our term is a single end-date (xsd:date) marking when an EPD expires, while the upstream term models a full validity Period (an interval with start/end) over generic Evidence; they overlap in the temporal-validity concept but differ in kind (point vs interval) and datatype, so neither true containment nor interchangeability holds.

findings.rationale: Our CAS Number property applies to any hazardous substance, whereas the upstream term refers specifically to a component’s CAS number.
findings.qaRationale: Both record the same CAS registry identifier so they overlap strongly, but they attach to different (and not cleanly nested) bearers — our hazardous-substance vs. their component — and the upstream term lacks a definition, so a clean BROAD subsumption can't be asserted.

findings.rationale: Our property captures only raw‑material acquisition emissions, whereas the upstream term represents the overall carbon footprint.
findings.qaRationale: Both are carbon-footprint properties, but ours scopes a single lifecycle stage (raw-material acquisition) which is a more granular slice of the upstream term's overall product carbon footprint.

findings.rationale: Our property specifically links a product to a carbon footprint declaration, whereas the upstream term refers generally to a carbon footprint value.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties relating a product to carbon footprint information and overlap substantially, but ours points to a structured declaration object while the undefined upstream term appears to denote a footprint value, so they overlap without a clean subset relation.

findings.rationale: Both properties link a product to its environmental product declaration, representing the same concept.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties linking a product to an environmental product declaration, but the upstream term has no definition while ours is constrained to EN 15804, so interchangeability cannot be confirmed and only overlap can be asserted.

findings.rationale: The candidate property provides general certification information, while OUR property specifically refers to the certification of a data provider.
findings.qaRationale: Both express certification, but ours is scoped specifically to the data provider (a kind of organization) whereas schema:hasCertification covers certification of any product, organization, service, place, or person, making ours a strict specialization.

findings.rationale: The OUR property refers specifically to a data provider’s certification, whereas the schema.org property is a general identifier for any certification instance.
findings.qaRationale: Our property captures a data provider's certification/accreditation status, while schema.org's certificationIdentification is the identifier string of a Certification instance for lookup/verification, a different concept rather than a narrower one.

findings.rationale: The candidate property covers general certification for products, organizations or locations, whereas OUR property is specifically about the certification of a data provider.
findings.qaRationale: Both are certification properties and a data provider is an organisation, so OUR term is a narrower, role-specific specialization of GS1's general certification of a product/organisation/location.

findings.rationale: The GS1 property is a general certification identifier for any product, party or location, while the OUR property specifically refers to certification of a data provider.
findings.qaRationale: Both concern certification of a party, but ours describes the data provider's accreditation status as a string while GS1's is a certificate-instance reference identifier for product/party/location, so they overlap without a clean subtype relation.

findings.rationale: The candidate property refers to any certification subject, while OUR property specifically denotes the certification of a data provider.
findings.qaRationale: gs1:certificationSubject points to the entity (object/party/location) that a certification applies to, whereas oec:dataProviderCertification is a string naming the data provider's own certification/accreditation, so they denote different concepts rather than a broader/narrower pair.

findings.rationale: Our property refers specifically to an impact indicator result within an environmental product declaration, whereas the upstream term denotes a generic indicator.
findings.qaRationale: A generic upstream 'indicator' property plausibly subsumes our EPD-specific impactIndicator, making ours the narrower term, but the upstream definition is absent so the conceptual match cannot be confirmed beyond name similarity.

findings.rationale: Our property captures a specific impact indicator result within an EPD, whereas the upstream term refers to general emissions performance.
findings.qaRationale: Our property is a generic LCA/EPD impact-indicator result (any impact category) while UNTP emissionsPerformance is a specific emissions/GHG performance claim, so they are not the same concept and ours is certainly not narrower than it.

findings.rationale: Both terms denote the same concept of a substance declared as a concern, with identical labels and intended use.
findings.qaRationale: Both are EU-DPP classes named SubstanceOfConcern denoting the same regulatory concept, but the upstream term carries no definition to confirm full interchangeability and our term adds SCIP/ESPR-specific scope, so a weaker overlap grade is warranted.

findings.rationale: Our class specifies substances of concern per SCIP/ESPR, whereas the upstream term is a generic substance.
findings.qaRationale: Both are classes; a substance of concern is a regulatory subset of the generic upstream Substance, so ours is narrower.

findings.rationale: The OUR class specifies hazardous substances of concern per SCIP/ESPR, which is a more specific subset of the general chemical substance described by schema.org.
findings.qaRationale: schema.org ChemicalSubstance is a pure-chemistry matter-composition concept (constant-composition molecular entities per ChEBI), whereas oec:SubstanceOfConcern is a regulatory hazard-declaration concept (SCIP/ESPR) defined by extrinsic regulatory status rather than chemical composition, so they are not the same concept and the match should be rejected.

findings.rationale: Our class specifies a particular type of hazardous substance (substance of concern) whereas schema.org Substance is a generic matter, making our term a narrower specialization.
findings.qaRationale: schema.org Substance is a generic class for any matter of defined composition, whereas oec:SubstanceOfConcern is a regulatory hazard-declaration concept (SCIP/ESPR) about a substance's restricted status rather than the substance itself, so they are not the same concept.

findings.rationale: Both properties capture the date of completion of an audit or report, but one is for due‑diligence reporting and the other for certification auditing.
findings.qaRationale: A due-diligence report date and a certification audit completion date describe different events in different domains (regulatory due diligence vs. certification auditing), so they are not the same concept.

findings.rationale: Both properties denote the reference standard used for an environmental product declaration.
findings.qaRationale: UNTP referenceStandard is a generic, undefined reference-standard property applicable across conformity contexts, whereas ours is specifically the reference standard and PCR version of an environmental product declaration, making our term narrower rather than interchangeable.

findings.rationale: Both properties record the name of a standard, but one refers to an EPD reference standard while the other is a general certification standard.
findings.qaRationale: An EPD reference standard / PCR version (EN 15804) governs how an environmental declaration is calculated and is not a product certification standard, so the overlap is only the shared word 'standard' rather than the underlying concept.

findings.rationale: Our class specifies ESPR product categories, which are a specific classification of products; the candidate represents any product, a more general concept.
findings.qaRationale: A product category is a classification of products, not a kind of product itself, so it is a distinct concept from gs1:Product (the product instance) rather than a narrower subclass of it.

findings.rationale: Both properties provide textual instructions for end‑of‑life handling of a product, but one focuses on dismantling while the other is specifically for consumer recycling.
findings.qaRationale: Dismantling instructions (how to disassemble a product, typically for repairers/recyclers) is a distinct concept from consumer-facing instructions for sorting product/packaging materials into recycling streams, and the ranges differ (document reference vs. plain text).

findings.rationale: Dismantling instructions are a specific type of end‑of‑life instruction, making our property narrower than the upstream endOfLifeInstructions.
findings.qaRationale: Dismantling is one specific end-of-life operation, so our property is a genuine narrower (subset) case of the upstream endOfLifeInstructions, both being properties.

findings.rationale: Both properties record the date an item was issued, though one refers to a digital product passport and the other to a ticket.
findings.qaRationale: Both are date-issued properties with range date, but schema:dateIssued is scoped to tickets/credentials while ours specifically records DPP issuance, so they overlap without being interchangeable.

findings.rationale: Both properties record the date an item was originally issued, though one refers to a digital product passport and the other to a certification.
findings.qaRationale: A passport's issuance date and a certification's original issuance date are different concepts about different artifacts, so they are not the same property despite both being issue dates.

findings.rationale: Both properties denote the date an item (passport or content) ceases to be valid, though the candidate is broader in scope.
findings.qaRationale: Both are expiry-date properties, but schema:expires is a general content/certification expiry whereas ours is the specific case of a Digital Product Passport expiring, making ours narrower.

findings.rationale: Both properties indicate the final date of validity for a document or certification, though one refers to a passport and the other to a generic certification.
findings.qaRationale: A DPP's expiry date and a certification's end-of-validity date share only the abstract 'end date' pattern but apply to fundamentally different subjects (a product passport document vs a conformity certification), so they are not the same concept.

findings.rationale: Our property provides a detailed recycled content breakdown, whereas the upstream term is a generic recycledContent property.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties about recycled content, but ours carries a structured pre/post-consumer breakdown (range oec:RecycledContent) while the upstream term is an undefined generic recycledContent property whose semantics and value shape are unknown, so they overlap without being interchangeable or cleanly nested.

findings.rationale: The candidate property refers to general sustainability and recycling information, whereas the OUR term specifies a detailed recycled content breakdown.
findings.qaRationale: gs1:sustainabilityInfo is a hyperlink property pointing to general sustainability/recycling documentation, whereas our term is a structured property carrying a quantified pre/post-consumer recycled-content breakdown, so they are different kinds of concept rather than a genuine broader/narrower pair.

findings.rationale: Both properties denote the media type (MIME format) of a document or file.
findings.qaRationale: Both denote a document's media type, but schema.org fileFormat is broader (it also permits a URL for niche formats and targets CreativeWork/MediaObject) while oec:mimeType is restricted to a MIME-type string on DocumentReference, so they overlap without being interchangeable.

findings.rationale: Our property specifies a URL for authorized repair service providers, which is a more specific use of the generic contact page concept.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties pointing to a web page for contact/service access, and ours is a specialized subset (authorized repair providers) of the generic upstream contact page, so it is narrower.

findings.rationale: Both properties are URLs, but the candidate is a generic website link whereas our property specifically points to authorized repair service providers.
findings.qaRationale: gs1:referencedFileURL is a generic catch-all link property with no repair semantics, so our domain-specific 'where to find authorized repair providers' property is a different concept, not a narrower child of it; the first pass even had the direction backwards (ours is more specific, never broader).

findings.rationale: Both terms denote the same class for circular economy performance, with identical scope and alignment to UNTP.
findings.qaRationale: Upstream gives no definition, so identity cannot be confirmed; our richly-scoped class only provably overlaps the bare UNTP CircularityPerformance label rather than being verifiably interchangeable.

findings.rationale: Both classes represent measures of circular economy, but the upstream term is a more generic indicator while ours specifies performance details.
findings.qaRationale: Our CircularityPerformance is an information aggregate spanning end-of-life, recycling, waste prevention and metrics, which is broader than a single CircularEconomyIndicator metric, though the upstream term's missing definition limits certainty.

findings.rationale: CircularityPerformance is a specific type of performance metric, thus it is narrower than the generic PerformanceMetric.
findings.qaRationale: Our term is an aggregate circularity-declaration section (explicitly aligned with UNTP's own CircularityPerformance class), whereas untp:PerformanceMetric is a single measured metric value, so it is a different concept rather than a narrower kind of metric.

findings.rationale: Performance Information focuses specifically on product performance and durability, whereas Product Information Page is a broader link to general product details.
findings.qaRationale: Ours is a structured performance/durability data property (range PerformanceInfo), while gs1:pip is a hyperlink to a consumer-facing product web page; they are different concept types, not a narrowing of the same concept.

findings.rationale: Our property refers specifically to certifications held by a facility, whereas the candidate property is a general certification attribute for any product, organization or location.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties asserting held certifications, but ours is restricted to facility (location/organisation) subjects whereas gs1:certification spans products, organisations, and locations, making ours a proper subset.

findings.rationale: hasCertification is a general property for any entity, while Facility Certifications specifically applies to facilities.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties asserting certifications on an entity, but ours is scoped specifically to facilities whereas schema:hasCertification ranges over products, organizations, services, places, and persons, making ours a narrower specialization.

findings.rationale: Certification Identification is a generic reference to any certificate, whereas Facility Certifications specifically applies to certificates held by a facility.
findings.qaRationale: Our property links a facility to full certification records (range gs1:CertificationDetails), whereas certificationIdentification is merely the identifier string of a single certificate instance; these are different concepts (the correct GS1 analogue would be gs1:certification, not its identifier).

findings.rationale: Facility Certifications is a specific property for facility-held certificates, whereas Certification Information is a general link to any certification data.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties linking to certification data, but oec:facilityCertifications is a facility-scoped specialization of gs1:certificationInfo's general certification link, making ours narrower.

findings.rationale: Facility Information covers all manufacturing or processing facilities, while Has Manufacturing Plant refers specifically to a physical plant.
findings.qaRationale: Both concern manufacturing facilities, but ours is an information/details container (range oec:FacilityInformation) covering manufacturing or processing, while gs1:manufacturingPlant links directly to a physical plant location, so they overlap without a clean broader/narrower subsumption.

findings.rationale: Recyclability Assessment is a specific type of evaluation, whereas Criterion represents any general condition for assessment.
findings.qaRationale: Our term is a class representing a completed evaluation of a product's recyclability (with a resulting grade/score), whereas m8g:Criterion models a reusable condition/requirement against which something is evaluated, not the assessment itself.

findings.rationale: Recyclability Assessment is a specific type of rating applied to product recyclability, whereas Rating is a general evaluation concept.
findings.qaRationale: Both are classes evaluating something, but a recyclability assessment is a domain-specific evaluative concept (yielding letter grades, scores, or rates) that overlaps with schema.org's generic numeric Rating without being a clean subtype of it, so it is closeMatch rather than a true narrower-than relation.

findings.rationale: Registration Number is a specific identifier for an official registration or license, whereas Entry in a register refers to any link to a register entry.
findings.qaRationale: Both concern official register identifiers, but ours is a literal string value (a registration/license number) while gs1:registryEntry is a link to a register entry covering broader cases (LEIs, location IDs), so they overlap without a clean narrower-than subsumption.

findings.rationale: Our property covers any official registration or license number, whereas the candidate refers specifically to a verification number for regulatory records.
findings.qaRationale: Our term is an operator's official registration/license identifier, whereas the GS1 term is an access-verification token for retrieving a regulatory record or dataset, so they denote different concepts rather than one being broader.

findings.rationale: Registration Number is a general official identifier, whereas vehicleIdentificationNumber refers to a specific type of registration number for vehicles.
findings.qaRationale: Our term is an operator's official registration/license number (domain: OperatorInformation), whereas a VIN is a manufacturer-assigned serial identifying a physical vehicle, a different concept on a different domain, not a sub-type of operator registration.

findings.rationale: Registration Number is a general official identifier, whereas membershipNumber refers to a specific type of registration number for memberships.
findings.qaRationale: An official registration or license number for an operator is a distinct concept from a membership identifier; membership is not a kind of regulatory registration, so the only link is superficial name/identifier similarity.

findings.rationale: Hazard Class is a specific classification of hazards, whereas Criterion is a generic condition for evaluation.
findings.qaRationale: A hazard classification category under CLP and a generic conformity-assessment criterion are unrelated concepts, not a genus-species pair, so no SKOS hierarchy holds.

findings.rationale: The economic operator responsible for a product includes manufacturers, distributors, importers, etc., whereas ‘Has Manufacturer’ refers only to the producing organization.
findings.qaRationale: Our 'economic operator responsible for the product' is a regulatory super-category that subsumes manufacturer along with importer, distributor and authorised representative, so ours is strictly broader than gs1:manufacturer (the producing organization only).

findings.rationale: Both properties represent the same concept of granularity level for a digital product passport.
findings.qaRationale: Both are DPP-level properties expressing passport granularity, but the upstream term lacks a definition and likely differs in encoding (UNTP typically uses a coded/enum value vs. our plain xsd:string token), so interchangeability cannot be confirmed.

findings.rationale: Both represent a container of data items, but DataElementCollection holds individual data elements while DataCatalog refers to datasets.
findings.qaRationale: DataElementCollection is a container of individual data elements (granular fields), whereas schema:DataCatalog is a collection of whole datasets, so they are different concepts at different levels of aggregation.

findings.rationale: Data Element Collection is a specific container for data elements, while Schema.org Collection is a general collection of items.
findings.qaRationale: schema:Collection aggregates creative works/products as content items, whereas oec:DataElementCollection is a metadata-structural container of DataElement definitions; only the word 'Collection' overlaps, not the concept.

findings.rationale: Both properties represent traceability performance metrics for supply chain verification.
findings.qaRationale: Both are DPP properties named traceabilityPerformance carrying supply-chain traceability metrics, but the upstream term has no published definition to confirm interchangeable scope, so equivalence cannot be asserted.

findings.rationale: The traceabilityPerformance property is a specific metric, whereas measuredProperty is a general property for any measurement.
findings.qaRationale: schema:measuredProperty is a structural meta-property identifying which property an Observation measures, not a traceability metric, so oec:traceabilityPerformance is not a narrower kind of it but a different concept.

findings.rationale: Both properties denote the same energy efficiency classification used in product passports.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties for a product's energy efficiency classification and the names align, but neither carries a definition to confirm identical value ranges/scales, so interchangeability cannot be asserted and the weaker grade is preferred.

findings.rationale: The candidate property specifies only the maximum energy‑efficiency class, whereas our property can refer to any energy‑efficiency class.
findings.qaRationale: schema:energyEfficiencyScaleMax denotes the top boundary of the rating scale for a product category, not the product's own assigned efficiency class that oec:energyEfficiencyClass records, so they are different concepts.

findings.rationale: Both properties denote the energy efficiency category/class of a product according to an international standard.
findings.qaRationale: schema:hasEnergyConsumptionDetails links a product to a full EnergyConsumptionDetails bundle (category plus min/max ratings and standard), whereas ours points directly to the single efficiency-class value, making ours narrower than this container property (schema:hasEnergyEfficiencyCategory would be the EXACT match).

findings.rationale: The candidate property specifies only the minimum energy‑efficiency class, whereas our property can refer to any energy‑efficiency class.
findings.qaRationale: schema:energyEfficiencyScaleMin denotes the worst-case bound of the regulated EU scale for a product category, not a product's assigned efficiency class, so it is a different concept rather than a broader version of ours.

findings.rationale: The upstream property defines a broader energy‑efficiency category that may include ratings or certifications, whereas the OUR term specifies a specific class within that category.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties expressing a product's energy-efficiency rating, but ours has a different domain (oec:EnergyEfficiency node vs schema's Product) and lacks a definition, so they overlap without being a clean subset relationship.

findings.rationale: The upstream property defines a generic numeric measurement value with unit code, whereas the OUR term specifies power consumption in active mode as that measurement.
findings.qaRationale: gs1:value is a generic atomic data-property holding a float on any QuantitativeValue, while oec:powerConsumptionOn is a domain-specific property linking EnergyEfficiency to a whole QuantitativeValue of on-mode power, so they are not the same concept.

findings.rationale: Both properties represent the same metric of the proportion of data derived from direct measurement versus estimates.
findings.qaRationale: Identical property name in a matching emissions context suggests the same metric, but the upstream term carries no definition to confirm the 0-1 direct-measurement-vs-estimate semantics are truly interchangeable, so a weaker grade is warranted.

findings.rationale: Both properties denote a version identifier for an object, but the schema.org property is generic to CreativeWork while ours is specific to a Digital Product Passport.
findings.qaRationale: Both are version-identifier properties of a resource, but schema.org scopes its property to CreativeWork while ours scopes to a Digital Product Passport, and a DPP is not unambiguously a CreativeWork, so they overlap without identity or a safe subsumption claim.

findings.rationale: Both properties denote a unique identifier assigned to a formal report or declaration by an issuing organization, though the specific domain (EPD vs general report) differs.
findings.qaRationale: schema:reportNumber identifies a schema:Report (a published creative work), not an Environmental Product Declaration's registration number, so the domains denote different concepts and the property is not interchangeable.

findings.rationale: The GS1 term denotes a general regulatory reference number, while our property specifies the registration number for an Environmental Product Declaration, making it a narrower, more specific concept.
findings.qaRationale: An EPD registration number is assigned by a voluntary environmental-declaration programme operator, not a regulatory information system/registry, so it is not a subtype of GS1's regulatory reference number despite superficial 'registration number' overlap.

findings.rationale: Both properties denote the same lifecycle boundary used for emissions calculations (Cradle‑to‑Gate or Cradle‑to‑Grave).
findings.qaRationale: Identical property name in the same emissions domain strongly suggests the same lifecycle-boundary concept, but the upstream term has no published definition to confirm interchangeable semantics, so EXACT is unwarranted.

findings.rationale: The OUR property specifies a particular reporting granularity for BatteryPass attributes, whereas the candidate represents a general granularity level.
findings.qaRationale: UNTP granularityLevel denotes passport-level granularity, which our ontology explicitly maps to the separate oec:granularityLevel term; oec:reportingGranularity is a distinct BatteryPass per-attribute concept, so this is the wrong target.

findings.rationale: Both properties provide a URL for returning or taking back a product, but the upstream term focuses on merchant returns while ours specifies consumer-facing take‑back program pages.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties giving a URL for product return/take-back, but schema.org's merchantReturnLink targets commercial merchant returns while ours denotes a consumer-facing end-of-life take-back program, so they overlap without being interchangeable.

findings.rationale: The candidate property specifies chemical composition (elements and ratios), which is a narrower, more specific type of material composition.
findings.qaRationale: Material composition (constituent materials, e.g. 80% cotton/20% polyester) and chemical composition (identity/ratio of chemical elements) describe overlapping but distinct axes of a substance's makeup, not a clean genus-species relation, so they are related yet not interchangeable.

findings.rationale: The candidate property describes textile material composition with percentages, a specific instance of the more general material composition concept represented by our property.
findings.qaRationale: Our generic material-composition property subsumes the textile-specific GS1 property, which is scoped to textile material content with percentages, so ours is broader.

findings.rationale: The candidate term refers specifically to packaging material composition quantity, whereas the OUR term covers all material compositions.
findings.qaRationale: Our term is a general material-composition object property (range oec:MaterialComposition), whereas the candidate is a packaging-specific quantity measure, differing in both domain (packaging) and nature (a quantity value, not a composition entity), so they are not the same concept.

findings.rationale: Our class represents the overall energy‑efficiency declaration of a product, which includes levels and certifications; the candidate is only the enumeration of those levels.
findings.qaRationale: schema:EnergyEfficiencyEnumeration is an enumeration type defining the value space of efficiency levels/certifications, whereas oec:EnergyEfficiency is a declaration record bundling rating, EPREL registration, and consumption modes; an enumeration is not subsumed by (nor equivalent to) a declaration, so the proposed BROAD relation misrepresents the semantic mismatch.

findings.rationale: Our class covers all EU energy efficiency classes plus EPREL registration and consumption modes, whereas the schema.org class enumerates only the EU energy efficiency classes.
findings.qaRationale: Our class is a declaration concept whose A-G class is just one facet (alongside EPREL registration and consumption modes), whereas schema.org's term is specifically the enumeration of EU energy-efficiency class values, so ours is the broader declaration only in scope but the schema.org type is a value enumeration that ours is not interchangeable with.

findings.rationale: The candidate class covers all energy‑consumption information for a product, while our class focuses specifically on the declared efficiency levels and certifications.
findings.qaRationale: Our class is the EU A-G/EPREL efficiency-declaration subset of schema.org's broader EnergyConsumptionDetails, which spans all regulation-based energy-consumption information for an energy-using product.

findings.rationale: Our class includes all EU energy efficiency classes, EPREL registration and consumption modes, whereas the schema.org class refers only to EnergyStar certification.
findings.qaRationale: The schema.org term is a value enumeration for US EnergyStar certification status, not a structured energy-efficiency declaration container, so despite the shared theme they are different kinds of concept rather than ours-broader.

findings.rationale: Both properties express a current status as a boolean, but one refers to general compliance while the other specifically concerns certification.
findings.qaRationale: Compliance status (a boolean of whether something conforms to requirements) is a distinct concept from certification status (the lifecycle state, e.g. active/inactive, of a certificate), so they are not the same property.

findings.rationale: Both classes describe product performance metrics, but the OUR term specifically includes lifespan and durability per ESPR Article 7, whereas the candidate is a more generic performance metric.
findings.qaRationale: Ours is an ESPR-scoped information container aggregating lifespan, usage cycles, and metrics, while the upstream is a single atomic performance metric, so they overlap conceptually but are not interchangeable nor cleanly broader/narrower.

findings.rationale: Performance Information is a specific subset of the broader concept of Reliability, focusing on lifespan and usage cycles.
findings.qaRationale: Reliability is a specific durability quality-attribute whereas oec:PerformanceInfo is a broad documentation container for lifespan/usage/performance metrics, so they are not the same concept and the first-pass NARROW is also backwards-directed.

findings.rationale: Performance Information encompasses durability plus additional performance metrics, whereas Durability refers only to the product’s endurance.
findings.qaRationale: Our PerformanceInfo covers durability plus broader performance metrics (lifespan, usage cycles), so durability is a proper subset, making ours broader; both are classes.

findings.rationale: Our property refers specifically to an EPR scheme registration number, whereas the candidate is a general link to any register entry.
findings.qaRationale: An EPR compliance-scheme producer registration number is a specific instance of the general 'entry in a register that acts as an alternative identifier' that gs1:registryEntry models, so ours is the narrower concept.

findings.rationale: Our property is a specific EPR scheme registration number, while the candidate covers any official business registration.
findings.qaRationale: schema:companyRegistration denotes a business's legal-entity/incorporation registration (Companies House, Chamber of Commerce) as a Certification object, whereas ours is a producer's identifier within an EPR environmental compliance scheme as a plain string — a different kind of registration, not a subtype.

findings.rationale: Our property is a specific identifier for a DataElement within its container, whereas the candidate is a generic identifier applicable to any Thing.
findings.qaRationale: oec:elementId is a specific kind of identifier (a container-scoped element id) and thus a proper subtype of schema:identifier's generic any-Thing identifier.

findings.rationale: Both properties are URLs, but reportUrl is specifically for a due diligence report, whereas referencedFileURL is a generic website reference.
findings.qaRationale: oec:reportUrl is a specialized slot for a due diligence report's URL, a proper subset of gs1:referencedFileURL's generic any-resource file reference, so ours is strictly narrower.

findings.rationale: Our property refers specifically to waste prevention information, while the candidate covers broader sustainability and recycling content.
findings.qaRationale: Our waste-prevention URL property is a specific subset of the candidate's broader sustainability-and-recycling link, so ours is narrower than the GS1 term.

findings.rationale: Both properties describe a time span related to data, but Data Retention Period is specific to regulatory retention rules while validity period refers more generally to evidence truthfulness.
findings.qaRationale: A regulatory data-retention duration (how long data must be kept) is a distinct concept from an evidence validity interval (when an assertion holds true), differing in meaning, domain (AccessRights vs Evidence), and range (a duration quantity vs a time interval).

findings.rationale: A Due Diligence Report is a specific form of compliance declaration, so it is narrower than the general ComplianceDeclaration concept.
findings.qaRationale: A due diligence report documents supply-chain risk investigation, which is a distinct concept from a compliance/conformity declaration asserting regulatory conformance, so ours is not a narrower kind of the candidate.

findings.rationale: A Due Diligence Report is a specific type of evidence that demonstrates compliance with supply‑chain transparency requirements, making it narrower than the generic Evidence concept.
findings.qaRationale: A due diligence report is a documentary artifact about supply-chain processes that can serve as CCCEV Evidence but is not inherently 'proof that a requirement is met,' so the concepts overlap functionally rather than forming a clean genus-species (NARROW) relation.

findings.rationale: Both classes categorize types of documents used as evidence, but EvidenceTypeList is a broader grouping for any evidence type, whereas Document Type focuses specifically on supporting documents.
findings.qaRationale: Ours is a class of supporting-document types for a passport, while EvidenceTypeList is a CCCEV grouping of evidence types proving conformance to a requirement, so the concepts do not align.

findings.rationale: Document Type is a specific category of supporting documents, whereas Evidence Type covers all kinds of evidence, making it broader.
findings.qaRationale: Both are document/evidence typology classes that overlap, but oec:DocumentType is a general taxonomy of DPP supporting files while CCCEV EvidenceType is evidence characterized against conformity requirements, so the relationship is overlapping rather than a clean subsumption.

findings.rationale: Both terms denote the same set of document type codes used to reference supporting files.
findings.qaRationale: Both are code-list classes enumerating document/file types and overlap heavily, but GS1's ReferencedFileTypeCode is scoped to referenced-file codes while ours is a generic DPP supporting-document type, so they are approximate rather than interchangeable.

findings.rationale: Both properties refer to the type/category of an impact indicator, though the upstream term lacks a specific definition.
findings.qaRationale: Ours is an EN 15804 LCA-specific property whose range is a controlled impact-category type; the upstream term is an undefined, generically-named 'indicator' property with no stated domain, range, or semantics to support any reliable cross-vocabulary equivalence.

findings.rationale: Repairability Information is a specific subset of durability, focusing on repair and maintenance aspects rather than overall endurance.
findings.qaRationale: Repairability and durability are distinct, sibling ESPR ecodesign parameters (Art. 5), not a subset relation; repair scores/spare-part/instructions are not a kind of durability, so they are not the same concept.

findings.rationale: The property covers any performance or efficiency class, whereas the candidate is specifically for energy efficiency.
findings.qaRationale: Our property covers any performance or efficiency class while the candidate is restricted to energy efficiency, so ours subsumes it (both are properties).

findings.rationale: Our property describes any performance class, while the candidate specifies only the maximum energy‑efficiency class.
findings.qaRationale: Our term records a product's assigned performance/efficiency class, whereas energyEfficiencyScaleMax specifies the upper boundary (most efficient class) of the regulated scale itself, not the product's own rating, so they are different concepts.

findings.rationale: Both properties define the energy efficiency class of a product according to an international standard.
findings.qaRationale: Both concern energy efficiency class, but ours is a generic string-valued performance/efficiency class spanning any product attribute while schema:hasEnergyConsumptionDetails is energy-specific and links to a structured EnergyConsumptionDetails object rather than carrying the class value itself, so they overlap without being interchangeable.

findings.rationale: Our property specifies a performance class rating (e.g., A+++ to G), whereas the candidate covers any energy‑efficiency category, including ratings or certifications.
findings.qaRationale: Our property covers any performance/efficiency class (energy labels being just one example), whereas the schema.org candidate is specifically the energy-efficiency category, making ours the broader concept rather than the narrower one.

findings.rationale: Our property specifies a Decentralized Identifier (DID), whereas the candidate is a general identifier for any Thing.
findings.qaRationale: A DID is one specific kind of identifier, so oec:did is a narrower property under schema:identifier's general identifier concept.

findings.rationale: Both properties represent a Decentralized Identifier (DID) used to link an entity to verifiable identity infrastructure.
