findings.qaRationale: The upstream candidate is the DID Core specification document URL, not a defined property term, so there is no property-to-property concept match to anchor a graded SKOS relation.

findings.rationale: Our property specifies a particular recyclability methodology, whereas the candidate is a general link to sustainability and recycling information.
findings.qaRationale: gs1:sustainabilityInfo is a generic link to sustainability/recycling informational content, whereas our term identifies the specific assessment standard/methodology applied (EN 15343, PEF), which is metadata about an assessment rather than the same concept as an info link.

findings.rationale: Our class covers only the EU A‑G energy efficiency classes, whereas the schema.org enumeration also includes A+, A++, and A+++. Thus our term is a narrower subset.
findings.qaRationale: Both classes model the same EU 2017/1369 energy-efficiency class enumeration, and our "downstream delegated acts" scope already covers the A+/A++/A+++ rescaling, so this is an overlapping near-equivalent concept rather than a strict subset as the first pass claimed.

findings.rationale: Our class specifies the EU Energy Labelling Regulation A‑G classes, whereas the candidate covers a wider range of international energy efficiency levels.
findings.qaRationale: Both are classes denoting energy-efficiency classification scales, but ours is restricted to the EU 2017/1369 A-G classes while schema:EnergyEfficiencyEnumeration spans multiple international energy-efficiency standards, making ours a proper subset.

findings.rationale: Our class specifies only the EU A‑G energy efficiency classes, whereas EnergyConsumptionDetails is a broader concept covering all information about a product’s energy consumption and efficiency.
findings.qaRationale: Our term is the A-G energy efficiency rating itself (schema.org models this as EUEnergyEfficiencyEnumeration), whereas EnergyConsumptionDetails is a structured information container bundling energy consumption data, so they are different kinds of concept rather than a genuine broader/narrower pair.

findings.rationale: Our property describes any hazard impact of a hazardous substance, whereas the schema.org property is limited to serious adverse outcomes in medical therapy.
findings.qaRationale: schema.org's seriousAdverseOutcome is a medical-therapy property describing life-threatening complications of a treatment, a different concept and domain from a generic textual description of a hazardous substance's hazard impact, so they are not the same property.

findings.rationale: Our property specifies a URL for a directory of physical collection points, which is a more specific use case than the generic URL property in schema.org.
findings.qaRationale: schema:url denotes the canonical address of the item itself, whereas our property points outward to a separate directory resource of collection points, so it is not a narrower form of the same concept.

findings.rationale: Our property specifies a URL for a directory of physical collection points, which is a more specific use case than the generic referencedFileURL that can point to any internet resource.
findings.qaRationale: Both are property-type URLs and ours points to a specific kind of internet resource (a collection-point directory), making it a genuine narrowing of GS1's generic referencedFileURL/Website rather than a separate concept.

findings.rationale: Both properties provide a numeric representation of a stage, but our property is an indicator value for a lifecycle module rather than the ordinal stage number.
findings.qaRationale: schema.org/stageAsNumber denotes an ordinal stage number (e.g. recipe step 3), whereas oec:stageValue is a quantitative indicator measurement for a lifecycle module in a declared unit — different concepts, not the same property.

findings.rationale: Our property is a numeric indicator value for a lifecycle stage, which is a more specific use of the generic measurement value defined in GS1.
findings.qaRationale: Both are unit-qualified numeric-value properties, and our stageValue is a domain-specific specialization of GS1's generic measurement value, making ours genuinely narrower.

findings.rationale: Our property specifies a numeric indicator for a lifecycle stage, which is a more specific use of the generic quantitative value concept defined by schema.org.
findings.qaRationale: Both are numeric value-carrier properties so they overlap, but schema:value is a generic structural slot on a value node rather than a measured lifecycle indicator, so this is approximate overlap rather than a clean subsumption of ours under it.

findings.rationale: Both properties represent the total lifecycle carbon footprint in kg CO2e, aligning with the UNTP definition.
findings.qaRationale: Both express a product carbon footprint value, but with no UNTP definition I cannot confirm identical scope, unit (kg CO2e), or that UNTP's term is a plain decimal total rather than a structured value, so interchangeability is unproven.

findings.rationale: Our property covers total lifecycle CO₂e, whereas the schema.org property refers specifically to vehicle emissions per km.
findings.qaRationale: schema.org emissionsCO2 is a vehicle's tailpipe emission rate (g/km), a fundamentally different concept from a product's total lifecycle carbon footprint (kg CO2e), so it is not a broader/narrower form of the same concept.

findings.rationale: The repair portal URL is a specific type of organization page, making it a narrower use than the general GS1 homepage link.
findings.qaRationale: gs1:homepage denotes an organisation's general information/landing page, whereas our term denotes a specialized regulatory repair-documentation portal (spare parts, dismantling, updates) on a different domain — these are distinct concepts, not a homepage subtype.

findings.rationale: The repair portal URL is a specific type of service information link, making it narrower than the general GS1 Service information property.
findings.qaRationale: Both are post-sale support links and overlap, but repair (spare-parts catalogue, dismantling, software updates for independent repairers under Right-to-Repair) is not strictly contained in GS1's 'service or maintenance instructions,' so the relation is overlapping rather than a clean genus-species narrowing.

findings.rationale: Access Rights is a general data‑control concept, while DigitalDocumentPermission refers specifically to file access for a person or group.
findings.qaRationale: DigitalDocumentPermission models a single permission grant for a person/group to a specific file, whereas oec:AccessRights is a regulatory access-control policy concept over DPP data, so despite the shared access theme they are not the same concept.

findings.rationale: Both properties denote the fraction of post‑consumer recycled content expressed as a decimal between 0 and 1.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties denoting the fraction of post-consumer recycled content, and the matching label and conventional 0-1 decimal semantics support interchangeability, though the upstream definition is absent.

findings.rationale: The candidate property denotes recycled content in general, while the OUR term specifies the post‑consumer fraction only.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties expressing recycled-content fraction, but ours restricts to the post-consumer subset whereas the untp term covers recycled content generally, making ours narrower.

findings.rationale: The upstream term refers to overall packaging recycled content, while our property specifies only post‑consumer recycled content.
findings.qaRationale: Our term scopes recycled content by source (post-consumer) with no packaging restriction, while the upstream term scopes it by application (packaging) regardless of pre/post-consumer origin — orthogonal axes with no subset relationship, so neither is a graded match.

findings.rationale: Both classes describe deposit‑return arrangements for packaging, but the GS1 term focuses on deposit details rather than the broader scheme participation.
findings.qaRationale: Our term is a regulatory scheme/program the product participates in, whereas the GS1 class models the monetary deposit detail data for returnable packaging—related domain but a different concept (program vs. deposit-amount record).

findings.rationale: Deposit-Return Scheme is a specific type of ConformityScheme, making it narrower.
findings.qaRationale: A deposit-return (take-back/EPR) scheme is not a conformity assessment scheme; despite the shared word 'scheme' they are distinct concepts, so our term is not a narrower kind of ConformityScheme.

findings.rationale: RepairProvider covers all authorised repair providers for any product, whereas AutoRepair is a specific type of repair business limited to cars.
findings.qaRationale: oec:RepairProvider is an authorised repair service provider organization (any product), while schema:AutoRepair is a car-repair business and the two are not the same concept; if anything ours would be broader, but the car-specific scope makes this a reject rather than a clean SKOS hierarchy match.

findings.rationale: Our class covers all authorised repair providers, while the candidate is a specific type of repair shop for motorcycles.
findings.qaRationale: schema:MotorcycleRepair is a narrow motorcycle-specific repair-shop business category, and pairing our generic authorised repair-provider concept with it is an arbitrary name-similarity artifact rather than a meaningful conceptual alignment.

findings.rationale: Both terms denote the same concept of greenhouse gas emissions data, aligned with UNTP EmissionsPerformance.
findings.qaRationale: Both are classes denoting a greenhouse-gas emissions data container and our term is explicitly modeled on UNTP's, but with no upstream definition to confirm field-level interchangeability the safer graded match is closeMatch rather than exactMatch.

findings.rationale: Both classes represent the same container for greenhouse gas emissions data, matching the UNTP EmissionsPerformance concept.
findings.qaRationale: The candidate IRI is OUR own term's IRI in the oec: namespace, not an independent upstream concept, so a cross-vocabulary SKOS mapping to itself is invalid.

findings.rationale: EmissionsPerformance is a specific type of PerformanceMetric, focusing on greenhouse gas emissions data.
findings.qaRationale: Our term is a container aggregating GHG emissions data (explicitly aligned with UNTP's own EmissionsPerformance), whereas PerformanceMetric is a generic atomic measurement concept, so they are not the same concept and the proper upstream anchor is EmissionsPerformance, not PerformanceMetric.

findings.rationale: EmissionsPerformance is a specific type of Performance, focusing on greenhouse gas data.
findings.qaRationale: Our emissions-specific container is one specialization of UNTP's generic abstract Performance class (which also subsumes circularity, traceability, etc.), so ours is properly narrower.

findings.rationale: EmissionsPerformance is a container for various emissions metrics, while CarbonFootprint represents a single metric.
findings.qaRationale: CarbonFootprint is a constituent metric of our EmissionsPerformance container, a part-whole relation rather than the hierarchical broader/narrower of the same concept that BROAD would require, so reject.

findings.rationale: EmissionsPerformance focuses specifically on greenhouse gas data, whereas EnvironmentalFootprint is a broader concept that can include multiple environmental impact categories.
findings.qaRationale: Both are classes, and our GHG-emissions-specific container is a proper subset of the broader multi-category EnvironmentalFootprint concept, so ours is narrower.

findings.rationale: passportIdentifier is a specific URI for a DPP instance, while schema.org identifier is a general property for any kind of identifier.
findings.qaRationale: oec:passportIdentifier is a specialized identifier property for a single DPP instance, semantically subsumed by schema.org's generic identifier property for any Thing, so ours is the narrower property.

findings.rationale: tradeItemPieceNumber is a specific sequential index for pieces of a trade item, while schema.org position denotes any item's place in an arbitrary sequence.
findings.qaRationale: oec:tradeItemPieceNumber is a domain-specific component index over physical pieces of a multi-piece trade item, whereas schema.org/position is a generic ordinal of an item within a series and is not the same concept, so reject rather than over-anchor on superficial sequence-numbering similarity.

findings.rationale: Both properties capture the language tag of a value using ISO 639 and ISO 3166-1 codes.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties named 'language', but the upstream term has no definition to confirm it carries the same ISO 639 + ISO 3166-1 language-tag semantics, so EXACT cannot be justified.

findings.rationale: Our property includes an ISO 639 language plus a country code, whereas the GS1 property specifies only an ISO 639‑1 language code.
findings.qaRationale: Our generic language-tag property (any value, language plus country subtag) is broader than GS1's fileLanguageCode, which is scoped specifically to the targeted language of a digital asset/file.

findings.rationale: Our property requires an ISO 639 language plus a country code, whereas the GS1 property specifies only an ISO 639‑1 language code.
findings.qaRationale: Our term is a generic language-tag annotation of any value's text, whereas gs1:availableLanguage denotes the language(s) a specific contact point operates in, so they are different concepts despite both referencing ISO 639.

findings.rationale: Both properties use IETF BCP 47 language tags (ISO 639 + region), but schema.org inLanguage refers to content or action, whereas our property is a language tag of an arbitrary value.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties asserting the language of something via standard language codes, but oec:language is a generic value-level language tag whereas schema:inLanguage scopes specifically to the language of content or an action, so they overlap without being interchangeable.

findings.rationale: Both use IETF BCP 47 language tags, but our property is a generic language tag for any value, whereas schema.org’s knowsLanguage applies specifically to persons or organizations.
findings.qaRationale: schema.org knowsLanguage asserts which languages a person or organization is competent in, whereas oec:language is a metadata language tag annotating the language of a text value — different concepts, not a broader/narrower pair.

findings.rationale: Both properties refer to consumer recycling guidance, but our property is a URL to instructions whereas GS1’s property holds the textual instructions themselves.
findings.qaRationale: GS1's property holds free-text consumer recycling instructions while ours is a URL pointing to broader end-of-life handling instructions; they are related but not interchangeable, and a URL-range property cannot be an EXACT/CLOSE peer of a text-range one, so from our perspective it reads as a narrower, differently-typed reference rather than the same concept.

findings.rationale: Both properties provide a URL for end‑of‑life handling instructions, matching in purpose and semantics.
findings.qaRationale: Both are end-of-life instruction properties with matching names, but the upstream term lacks any definition or stated range so I cannot confirm it is a URL-only property interchangeable with ours, which is explicitly constrained to xsd:anyURI.

findings.rationale: Both refer to product instructions, but the GS1 property covers general usage while our term is specifically for end‑of‑life handling.
findings.qaRationale: Our term is an end-of-life handling instructions URL on CircularityPerformance, whereas gs1:consumerUsageInstructions is free-text product usage guidance for the use phase, a different lifecycle concept and different value type.

findings.rationale: The GS1 sustainabilityInfo property links to general sustainability and recycling information, whereas our end‑of‑life instructions specifically provide a URL for end‑of‑life handling.
findings.qaRationale: Our end-of-life handling instructions URL is a specific subset of GS1's broad sustainability-and-recycling information link, so ours is narrower than the upstream property.

findings.rationale: repairProviderName specifies the provider’s name, whereas contactPoint is a broader property for all main contact information.
findings.qaRationale: Our term is a literal string holding the provider's name, whereas m8g:contactPoint is an object property linking to full contact-information resources; a name is not a sub-type of contact point, so they are not the same concept.

findings.rationale: Our class refers to any external document, while the candidate is a specific type of reference for APIs.
findings.qaRationale: schema:APIReference is a CreativeWork that IS API documentation content, whereas oec:DocumentReference is a pointer/reference to an external document, so they are not the same concept and the apparent overlap is only nominal.

findings.rationale: Our DocumentReference encompasses any external document (reports, manuals, guides) in multiple languages and formats, whereas ReferencedFileDetails is a narrower class that specifically provides URL and file‑specific information.
findings.qaRationale: Both are classes pointing to an external file/document with URL and metadata, but neither is clearly broader since GS1's ReferencedFileDetails covers any electronic file (not only documents) while ours is document-scoped yet adds language handling, so they overlap without one subsuming the other.

findings.rationale: DocumentReference is a specific type of document that references external resources with language and format support, whereas FOAF Document is a general document concept.
findings.qaRationale: Our term is a reference/pointer to an external document (with language and format metadata), a distinct concept from foaf:Document which is the document itself, so they are not the same concept.

findings.rationale: DocumentReference covers any external document, while NoteDigitalDocument is a specific type of digital note.
findings.qaRationale: NoteDigitalDocument is a personal authoring note, not a general external-document reference covering reports/certificates/manuals, so they are not the same concept (the relevant schema.org peer would be DigitalDocument).

findings.rationale: Both classes represent reported environmental impact values, but the upstream term lacks the explicit indicator type and unit details of our definition.
findings.qaRationale: Our term models a single named impact indicator's result (type, unit, value, per-module breakdown), whereas the undefined upstream 'ImpactValues' reads as a plural container of impact values, a cardinality/scope mismatch that makes them different concepts.

findings.rationale: Both properties indicate the specific version of a schema that an instance conforms to, using a string or URL.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties expressing which schema/standard version an instance conforms to, but schema.org's is a generic CreativeWork property oriented to schema.org releases while ours is bound to the DPP and the EN 18223 'dppSchemaVersion' concept, so they overlap strongly without being strictly interchangeable.

findings.rationale: Both properties record a version identifier, but the DPP Schema Version is specific to DigitalProductPassport instances, whereas schema.org’s version applies generally to any CreativeWork.
findings.qaRationale: oec:dppSchemaVersion records a version identifier like schema.org/version but is constrained to the schema/standard a DigitalProductPassport conforms to, a narrower instance of the general CreativeWork version property.

findings.rationale: HazardousSubstance is a specific type of chemical substance that meets EU CLP criteria, making it narrower than the general ChemicalSubstance.
findings.qaRationale: Our term is a CLP hazard-classification declaration (covering substances and mixtures, framed as a regulatory record), not a subtype of schema's 'portion of matter of constant composition', so it is a different concept rather than a narrower one.

findings.rationale: The upstream term Substance is a generic class of any substance, whereas our HazardousSubstance refers specifically to substances declared under EU CLP Regulation.
findings.qaRationale: Both are classes and our hazardous-substance concept (CLP-declared) is a proper subset of the upstream generic Substance class, so ours is narrower.

findings.rationale: Both properties refer to the presence of hazardous substances in a product, but the candidate lacks the explicit range to HazardousSubstance.
findings.qaRationale: untp:hazardous reads as a boolean hazard flag on a product, whereas oec:hazardousSubstances enumerates structured HazardousSubstance entities present, so they are different concepts rather than a graded match.

findings.rationale: Both properties provide a human‑readable description of an item, but our term is specific to trade item pieces while schema.org’s description is generic.
findings.qaRationale: schema.org's generic 'A description of the item' is conceptually equivalent to but broader-purpose than our piece-specific description; from our term's perspective ours is the narrower specialization, making the upstream a broader match (our NARROW / their BROAD), though both are string-valued descriptive properties so the relation is graded not exact.

findings.rationale: tradeItemPieceDescription is a general human‑readable description of any trade item piece, while textileMaterialDescription refers specifically to the material within a product.
findings.qaRationale: Ours names a physical piece/article of a multi-part trade item (Jacket, Left shoe), while gs1:textileMaterialDescription names a constituent material (Rayon); these are unrelated concepts, not a broader/narrower pair.

findings.rationale: tradeItemPieceDescription refers to a description of an individual trade item piece, which is a more specific concept than the general product description provided by Product Description.
findings.qaRationale: Both are human-readable text descriptions, but ours describes a constituent piece of a multi-piece trade item (e.g. 'Left shoe') rather than the brand-and-descriptor product description GS1 intends, so they overlap without one cleanly subsuming the other.

findings.rationale: The upstream term describes any component location in a product, while our Substance Location specifically denotes the location where a particular substance is present.
findings.qaRationale: Both are string properties locating a constituent within a product, but ours locates a substance of concern while theirs locates a component, which are different entity types, so NARROW is unjustified and only an approximate overlap holds.

findings.rationale: The property describes the location of a specific substance within a product, whereas the upstream ‘location’ is a generic link to any location.
findings.qaRationale: locn:location is a generic geospatial link to a Location resource, whereas oec:substanceLocation is a string naming the component/part within a product where a substance occurs — a different concept with an incompatible range, not a narrowing of geospatial location.

findings.rationale: Both properties express the role of an economic operator or organization, though the upstream term uses a generic party/organization context.
findings.qaRationale: Both are role-assignment properties, but ours is constrained to economic-operator roles (manufacturer, importer) over OperatorInformation, a specialization of GS1's generic any-party organizationRole.

findings.rationale: The upstream role property applies to any agent in a participation, whereas our operatorRole specifically denotes the role of an economic operator.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties expressing the function/role an actor plays; ours restricts the generic agent-in-participation role to specifically the economic operator's role, making it a genuine narrowing of the upstream term.

findings.rationale: Both properties denote the monetary deposit amount for a returnable package, expressed as a quantitative value with currency.
findings.qaRationale: Both denote a refundable monetary deposit, but GS1's term is scoped to an individual returnable package while ours is the point-of-sale deposit within a DepositReturnScheme, so they overlap heavily without being interchangeable.

findings.rationale: Both properties refer to substances considered regulatory concerns present in a product, though our definition specifies database requirements and explicit presence.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties flagging product-contained substances of regulatory concern, but GS1's 'ingredient' (intentionally-added component, claim-based) and our 'substance' (any SVHC present per SCIP/ESPR) frame overlapping-not-identical scopes, so CLOSE rather than EXACT.

findings.rationale: Both properties represent a list of substances identified as concerns in the product, matching in scope and intent.
findings.qaRationale: Both are DPP properties for substances of concern and almost certainly align, but the upstream term has no definition to confirm identical scope (e.g. SCIP/ESPR framing, value structure), so EXACT cannot be safely asserted.

findings.rationale: The candidate refers specifically to substances of concern in packaging, which is a narrower subset of all product substances.
findings.qaRationale: Both are substances-of-concern properties sharing SCIP/ESPR lineage, but ours scopes to the product article while the candidate is restricted to packaging, a distinct referent rather than a clean subset, so they overlap without one cleanly subsuming the other.

findings.rationale: Both properties record a date related to compliance activities, but one records verification while the other records audit completion.
findings.qaRationale: A generic compliance-verification date is not the same concept as the completion date of a certification audit; the events recorded differ and name overlap does not make them interchangeable.

findings.rationale: Both properties denote a date related to certification or compliance, but one specifies the start of validity while the other records when verification occurred.
findings.qaRationale: A compliance-verification date records when conformity was checked, whereas certificationStartDate marks the first date a certification is valid; these denote distinct events and are not the same concept.

findings.rationale: Both properties record the date of a compliance-related audit or verification, but one specifies a general compliance check while the other explicitly references a certification audit.
findings.qaRationale: Both are date properties marking a compliance/audit verification event, overlapping in meaning but not interchangeable since auditDate is scoped to a certification's last audit while complianceDate generically records compliance verification.

findings.rationale: Both properties denote the date on which a document or ticket is issued.
findings.qaRationale: Both denote an issuance date, but schema.org dateIssued is scoped to Ticket while ours applies to DocumentReference, so the concepts overlap without being interchangeable.

findings.rationale: Our property applies to any document’s issue date, whereas the candidate is limited to legislation adoption dates.
findings.qaRationale: A generic document issue/publication date is a different concept from a legislation's adoption-or-signature date, which is domain-specific to legislation and semantically distinct from issuance, so this is not the same property.

findings.rationale: The issue date of a document is a general concept, while the initial certification date refers specifically to the issuance of a certification.
findings.qaRationale: A generic document issue date over DocumentReference is not the same concept as a certification's original-issuance date carrying recertification-cycle semantics, so it is a name-adjacent mismatch rather than a broader/narrower pairing.

findings.rationale: Our property applies to any document’s issue date, whereas the candidate refers only to the audit completion date for certification.
findings.qaRationale: A generic document issue date and the completion date of a certification audit are distinct concepts (issuance versus audit completion), not a broader/narrower pair.

findings.rationale: Both properties denote the fraction of recycled material in a product, expressed as a decimal between 0 and 1.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties for a product's recycled-material fraction, but the upstream term has no published definition to confirm 1:1 interchangeability and UNTP commonly encodes such measures differently (e.g. percentage rather than our 0-1 decimal), so an EXACT claim is unsafe.

findings.rationale: Both properties refer to the proportion of recycled material in a product, but the upstream term lacks explicit scale or unit details.
findings.qaRationale: Recycled content (fraction made from recycled material) and recyclableContent (fraction that can be recycled at end-of-life) are distinct circularity concepts despite the near-identical name.

findings.rationale: Both properties represent the fraction of recycled material in a product, expressed as a decimal between 0 and 1.
findings.qaRationale: Both are decimal properties for the recycled-content fraction, but ours is an aggregate 'total' fraction with unspecified basis while the upstream term is explicitly a mass fraction (and lacks a definition to confirm identical scope/aggregation), so they overlap without being safely interchangeable.

findings.rationale: The candidate property describes any government‑assigned organization ID, whereas the OUR term specifies a single EU‑wide economic operator identifier.
findings.qaRationale: Both are organization-identifier properties, and our EU-specific EOID is a narrower instance of GS1's general government/trade-assigned organization ID, so ours is narrower than the candidate (the first pass's rationale supports NARROW, not BROAD).

findings.rationale: Both describe a time span measured in years, but one refers to product technical life while the other specifies warranty duration.
findings.qaRationale: Warranty duration is a commercial/legal promise period whereas technical lifetime is an engineering expectation of functional life; they share only the dimension of time-in-years, not the underlying concept.

findings.rationale: The candidate property defines a generic unit code, whereas the OUR term specifies a technical lifetime value that uses such a unit.
findings.qaRationale: schema:unitCode denotes the unit-of-measure code attached to a quantitative value, whereas oec:technicalLifetime denotes the product's expected technical lifespan; they are unrelated concepts, not a broader/narrower pair.

findings.rationale: The OUR property covers multiple industry classification systems (ISIC, NACE, NAICS), whereas the candidate property refers only to NAICS.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties, and our term generalizes across multiple industry classification schemes (ISIC, NACE, NAICS) while schema:naics is specifically the NAICS code, making ours strictly broader.

findings.rationale: Our property covers multiple industry classification schemes (ISIC, NACE, NAICS), whereas the candidate is specific to ISIC v4.
findings.qaRationale: Our property generically captures any industry-activity classification code (ISIC, NACE, NAICS), whereas schema:isicV4 is restricted to the single ISIC Revision 4 scheme, so ours properly subsumes the candidate.

findings.rationale: Our property covers multiple industry classification codes (ISIC, NACE, NAICS), whereas the candidate refers only to ISIC.
findings.qaRationale: The candidate IRI is the landing page for the UN ISIC classification scheme itself, not a comparable data property, so it is a different kind of entity than our property holding a classification code value and cannot stand in a property-to-property graded match.

findings.rationale: Biodegradability is a specific measurement result, whereas Criterion represents any condition for evaluation.
findings.qaRationale: Our Biodegradability is an observed measurement result for a material, whereas SEMICeu Criterion is a generic condition for evaluation/assessment; they are different concepts, so this is not even a narrow-of relation.

findings.rationale: Both properties record a date/time when data was updated or published, but the candidate refers specifically to structured‑data generation rather than a generic DPP data update.
findings.qaRationale: Both are record-level metadata timestamps about the data itself rather than the product, but ours marks the last update of DPP data generically while sdDatePublished specifically marks when the current structured-data serialization was generated/published, so they overlap without being interchangeable.

findings.rationale: Both properties record the date of the most recent review or update of content, though one refers to DPP data and the other to a web page.
findings.qaRationale: Our term is a data-modification timestamp (xsd:dateTime of last DPP data update), whereas schema:lastReviewed is a date of human content review for accuracy/completeness on a web page; these are distinct concepts (modification vs. review), so they do not match.

findings.rationale: Both terms denote the same concept of granularity level for a passport, with identical labels and implied scope.
findings.qaRationale: Both are classes modeling passport granularity (model/batch/item), but ours is specifically scoped to EN 18223 with EPCIS4DPP-derived tokens while the upstream UNTP class has no published definition to confirm true interchangeability, so a CLOSE match is safer than EXACT.

findings.rationale: Both classes describe the granularity of a product identifier (model, batch, or item), though our definition is specific to EN 18223 and EPCIS4DPP.
findings.qaRationale: Both are classes enumerating the same model/batch/item granularity tristate, but ours is framed as passport-level granularity anchored to EN 18223 and EPCIS4DPP Digital Link AIs while UNTP's is product-identifier granularity, so they overlap without being interchangeable.

findings.rationale: Both properties provide a URL for redeeming an item, but the deposit redemption channel is specific to container returns while the offer redemption URL targets online offers.
findings.qaRationale: gs1:offerRedemptionURL is for redeeming a promotional/marketing offer online, an entirely different concept from a deposit-return-scheme container-return directory; the shared word 'redemption' is name similarity, not conceptual overlap.

findings.rationale: Our property specifies a return link for deposit redemption channels, which is a more specific instance of the generic merchantReturnLink defined in schema.org.
findings.qaRationale: merchantReturnLink is a merchant's commercial product-return policy page (domain MerchantReturnPolicy), whereas ours is a public directory of deposit-return collection points for container refund under a deposit return scheme; these are distinct concepts, not a generic/specific pair.

findings.rationale: Our property specifies a URL for a document, while the GS1 property refers to any internet resource; thus our term is a narrower specialization.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties supplying a URL; ours is constrained to a document reference while GS1 referencedFileURL covers any internet resource (documents, people, etc.), so our term is a narrower specialization.

findings.rationale: Both properties provide a URL to an external file or webpage, but the GS1 term is specifically tied to product information whereas our term is a generic document reference.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties linking to an external file by URL, but gs1:referencedFile is specifically about additional product information whereas oec:documentUrl generically references any document, making our term broader than the GS1 candidate.

findings.rationale: Our property applies to any document URL, whereas the GS1 property is a specific subtype for certification agency URLs.
findings.qaRationale: gs1:certificationAgencyURL links to the issuing organisation's website (e.g. msc.org), not to a document, so it is not a narrower kind of oec:documentUrl but a distinct concept.

findings.rationale: Our property specifies a URL that points to a document reference, whereas schema.org’s url is a general link for any item.
findings.qaRationale: Both are URL-valued properties, but schema.org/url denotes the item's own canonical URL whereas oec:documentUrl is a link to a referenced document file (better anchored to gs1:referencedFileURL), so the overlap is approximate rather than a clean subsumption.

findings.rationale: Our property is a generic URL for any document, whereas the GS1 term refers specifically to URLs linking to company filing information.
findings.qaRationale: Our generic document-reference URL and GS1's company-filing-details URL are distinct concepts with unrelated domains; our term's true GS1 analog is gs1:referencedFileURL, not companyFilingURL.

findings.rationale: Our property points to a specific URL for separate collection requirements, which is a subset of the broader sustainability and recycling information link.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties linking to external information; separate-collection requirements are one specific subset of GS1's broader sustainability-and-recycling link, so ours is narrower.

findings.rationale: Our property refers to a specific URL for separate collection requirements, which is a narrower subset of the broader sustainability and recycling information link.
findings.qaRationale: The upstream target is an explicitly deprecated GS1 term (superseded by gs1:sustainabilityInfo) and is a broad sustainability/recycling info link rather than the specific separate-collection-requirements URL our property denotes, so it is not a valid anchor.

findings.rationale: Both properties are named utilityFactor and represent the same durability‑relative metric.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties plausibly in the same circularity/durability domain, but the upstream term has no definition so interchangeability (EXACT) cannot be confirmed and our specific 'relative to industry average' framing may diverge from UNTP's usage.

findings.rationale: Both terms denote the same measurement of a product’s carbon emissions.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties about a product's carbon footprint, but the upstream term lacks any definition and UNTP's carbonFootprint typically anchors a richer structured declaration rather than our single gs1:QuantitativeValue measurement, so interchangeability cannot be confirmed.

findings.rationale: Our class covers facility identification, location, and certification, while the candidate focuses only on linking identifiers to locations.
findings.qaRationale: Our class is a facility (a gs1:Place subtype carrying identification, location, and certification), whereas gs1:LocationID_Details is a helper structure for cross-linking disparate location identifiers to a GLN, so they are different concepts rather than a broader/narrower pair.

findings.rationale: Both classes represent a manufacturing or processing facility, but our definition includes ESPR‑specific attributes and GS1 Digital Link usage.
findings.qaRationale: Our ESPR-specific FacilityInformation, scoped to manufacturing/processing facilities with regulatory certification data, is a specialized subtype of UNTP's generic Facility class, so ours is narrower.

findings.rationale: Both classes describe a manufacturing or processing facility, but our class specifies ESPR‑based information and GS1 identifiers.
findings.qaRationale: Both are classes denoting a manufacturing/processing facility, overlapping in concept, but ours is an ESPR-scoped information record keyed to GS1 identifiers rather than UNTP's generic Facility entity, so they are related but not interchangeable.

findings.rationale: Both classes refer to a manufacturing or processing facility, but our class specifies ESPR‑based details and GS1 identifiers.
findings.qaRationale: Both are facility classes, but ours is an ESPR-scoped information container inheriting from gs1:Place while the upstream's lack of a definition leaves exact scope unverifiable, so overlapping-not-identical is the safest grade.

findings.rationale: Facility Information is a specific type of organization (manufacturing/processing facility) identified by GLN, whereas Organization covers all entities.
findings.qaRationale: Our term models a physical facility/site (explicitly inheriting gs1:Place properties for GLN/name/address) whereas gs1:Organization is a legal or physical entity/party, so despite both using a GLN they are distinct concepts (place vs. party), not a genus-species relation.

findings.rationale: Our class represents a specific type of place (manufacturing or processing facility) with additional attributes, making it narrower than the general schema.org Place.
findings.qaRationale: Our class models a manufacturing/processing facility as a gs1:Place subtype, which is a specific kind of the general schema:Place, so ours is narrower.

findings.rationale: Our class represents a facility with identification, location, and certification, whereas the candidate is only an address representation; thus our concept is broader.
findings.qaRationale: Our term is a facility (a physical site/place akin to gs1:Place) whereas locn:Address is merely an address representation that a facility has as one attribute; this is a has-a relation, not a broader/narrower form of the same concept.

findings.rationale: Our class includes facility identification, location, and certification, whereas the upstream class represents a generic location.
findings.qaRationale: Both denote an identifiable place, but our facility concept bundles identification and certification data beyond pure geography and is anchored on gs1:Place rather than being a clean subtype of the generic locn:Location, so the concepts overlap without strict subsumption.

findings.rationale: Facility Information is a specific type of Place, thus it is narrower than the generic Place class.
findings.qaRationale: A manufacturing/processing facility is a specialized subtype of the generic gs1:Place (and explicitly inherits gln/name/address from it), so our class is narrower than the upstream class.

findings.rationale: FacilityInformation encompasses identification, location, and certification, whereas Geometry only specifies spatial coordinates; thus our class is broader.
findings.qaRationale: FacilityInformation is a facility/Place concept while locn:Geometry is a spatial-coordinate shape; a facility is not a broader generalization of a geometry but a different kind of concept, so there is no graded subsumption match.

findings.rationale: Both properties represent the ISO 639‑1 language code for a digital asset, with identical scope and definition.
findings.qaRationale: Both encode an ISO 639-1 language code, but ours applies to a DocumentReference while gs1:fileLanguageCode targets a digital asset/file, so they overlap closely without being strictly interchangeable.

findings.rationale: Both classes represent the level of detail or granularity for DPP data, matching the ESPR definition.
findings.qaRationale: oec:AccessLevel concerns data access-permission tiers under ESPR Art. 9, whereas untp GranularityLevel concerns the level of data aggregation/detail, so despite the similar 'level' naming they are unrelated concepts.

findings.rationale: Compostability is a specific type of certification, whereas Certification is a general class for any official attestation.
findings.qaRationale: Compostability is a material end-of-life classification/category (the characteristic being attested), not a type of official attestation document, so it is not a subclass of schema.org/Certification despite the name overlap.

findings.rationale: Both properties link a statement or requirement to the law/regulation it is based on, but our property is specifically for due‑diligence reports.
findings.qaRationale: Ours names which regulation a due-diligence report asserts against (a regulatory-context value on a report), whereas m8g:isDerivedFrom links a CCCEV Requirement to its source framework; different concept, different domain, so not the same property.

findings.rationale: Our property specifies the exact regulation a due‑diligence statement refers to, whereas the GS1 property is a general link to any applicable regulatory information.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties whose object is regulatory information, but GS1's links a trade item to its broadly-applicable regulations while ours links a DueDiligenceReport to the single regulation it asserts conformity against — overlapping regulatory-anchor semantics with a different domain and relationship intent, not a clean subproperty subsumption.

findings.rationale: Operator Information specifies economic operator roles (manufacturer, importer, distributor) identified by GLN, which is a more specific subset of the broader GS1 Organization concept.
findings.qaRationale: Both are classes; our term scopes the broad gs1:Organization down to the regulatory subset of economic operators (manufacturer/importer/distributor) identified by GLN, making ours strictly narrower.

findings.rationale: Our class specifies economic operator roles (manufacturer, importer, distributor) whereas the upstream Party is a generic entity.
findings.qaRationale: OperatorInformation is a role-constrained subtype of party (economic operators only) inheriting from gs1:Organization, so it is narrower than the generic untp:Party class.

findings.rationale: OperatorInformation is a specific type of Actor that represents economic operators in EU regulations, making it narrower than the generic Actor class.
findings.qaRationale: Both are classes for supply-chain parties and our economic-operator concept (manufacturer/importer/distributor, identified by GLN as a gs1:Organization) is a specific subtype of the generic, undefined upstream Actor.

findings.rationale: Operator Information is a specialized class of economic operators (manufacturers, importers, distributors), which are specific types of Organization.
findings.qaRationale: OperatorInformation inherits from gs1:Organization and represents economic operators (manufacturer, importer, distributor), a regulatory subset of the very general schema:Organization, so ours is narrower.

findings.rationale: Our class specifies economic operator roles (manufacturer, importer, distributor) which are a subset of the broader concept of a legal person.
findings.qaRationale: Both model the supply-chain actor/organization and overlap substantially, but ours is an operator-information container scoped to economic-operator roles while LegalPerson is a legal-entity type (excluding natural-person operators), so the overlap is approximate rather than a clean subset.

findings.rationale: OperatorInformation includes all economic operator roles, while ManufacturerRecord is a specific subset for manufacturers.
findings.qaRationale: Our OperatorInformation class covers all economic operator roles (manufacturer, importer, distributor), so it subsumes a manufacturer-specific record, though the upstream term's missing definition limits certainty.

findings.rationale: OperatorInformation represents specific economic operator roles, whereas LegalEntity is a general legal entity concept.
findings.qaRationale: An economic operator is a legal entity acting in specific regulated roles (manufacturer, importer, distributor), making OperatorInformation a class-to-class hyponym of the general LegalEntity concept.

findings.rationale: Repair Provider is a specific type of service provider, thus it is narrower than the general schema.org provider.
findings.qaRationale: Both are provider-type properties, but schema:provider links a service/goods to the party that provides or produces it, whereas oec:repairProvider links a product to a third-party repair entity, so the relation is overlapping rather than a clean specialization.

findings.rationale: Both terms denote the same concept of a product lifecycle carbon footprint declaration.
findings.qaRationale: The upstream IRI is identical to OUR term's own IRI (oec:CarbonFootprintDeclaration), so this is a self-reference, not a cross-vocabulary alignment, and asserting a graded SKOS match to itself is meaningless.

findings.rationale: Our class represents a specific declaration (observation) of a product’s lifecycle carbon footprint, whereas the upstream class denotes the general concept of a carbon footprint.
findings.qaRationale: Both are carbon-footprint classes but ours is specifically a lifecycle-staged declaration/observation artifact while the undefined upstream class denotes the footprint concept itself, so they overlap rather than nest as a clean subtype.

findings.rationale: CarbonFootprintDeclaration is a specific type of environmental footprint focused solely on carbon, whereas EnvironmentalFootprint is a broader concept covering all environmental impacts.
findings.qaRationale: Both are classes in the footprint domain and carbon footprint is a subset of environmental footprint, but ours is an observation/declaration artifact scoped to carbon while the upstream (undefined) term denotes the broader environmental-impact footprint itself, so they overlap rather than sit in a clean narrower-than subsumption.

findings.rationale: The depositReturnScheme property refers to a product’s participation in a return‑deposit scheme, whereas hasReturnablePackageDepositDetails specifies the refund amounts for that package; thus our term is a broader concept.
findings.qaRationale: Both concern returnable-deposit packaging, but ours links a product to the scheme it participates in while GS1's links to regional refund-amount details, so they overlap without a clean subsumption.

findings.rationale: Deposit-Return Scheme is a specific type of recycling scheme that includes deposit, whereas Packaging Recycling Scheme covers all recycling schemes.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties keyed off packaging end-of-life schemes and overlap for deposit-bearing recyclable packaging, but a deposit-return scheme also covers reuse/refill (not only recycling) while gs1:packagingRecyclingScheme is specifically about recycling routing, so neither cleanly subsumes the other.

findings.rationale: depositReturnScheme refers to the overall scheme a product participates in, while returnablePackageDepositRegion specifies only the geographic region of that deposit; thus our term is a broader concept.
findings.qaRationale: Our property links a product to the deposit-return scheme entity it participates in, whereas the GS1 property identifies the geographic region tied to a deposit amount; these are distinct relationships with disjoint ranges, not a broader/narrower pair.

findings.rationale: Both represent a collection of codes that define a classification system, though CategoryCodeSet is generic while CustomsCommodityCodeType is specific to customs codes.
findings.qaRationale: Both are classes denoting a scheme/collection of codes, and our customs-commodity classification system is a domain-specific specialization of schema.org's generic CategoryCodeSet, so ours is narrower.

findings.rationale: Our term specifies a classification system for customs commodity codes, whereas the upstream Type Code is a generic code list.
findings.qaRationale: A customs-commodity-code classification scheme is a specific kind of the generic GS1 code-list type, so ours is a narrower specialization, though the upstream class is an abstract placeholder making the anchor weak.

findings.rationale: Both properties represent the length of a manufacturer’s warranty, using the same unit codes.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties measuring a warranty-related time span in the same units, but guaranteed minimum product lifespan (an ESPR durability metric) is not strictly interchangeable with the schema.org warranty-promise duration, so they overlap without being identical.

findings.rationale: RecycledContent specifies the declared content of recycled material, whereas RecycledMaterialsUse is a broader concept that includes any use of recycled materials.
findings.qaRationale: Our term is a structured content declaration (with pre-/post-consumer splits), whereas the undefined upstream RecycledMaterialsUse appears to model the act/usage of recycled materials, and with no upstream definition a defensible graded subsumption cannot be asserted.

findings.rationale: Recycled Content is a specific aspect of Packaging details, making it a narrower subclass.
findings.qaRationale: Recycled material content declaration is a distinct concept from packaging details and is not a subclass of packaging, since recycled content applies to product materials broadly rather than being an aspect of packaging.

findings.rationale: The identityCredentialUrl property specifies a URL for a verifiable credential, whereas referencedFileURL is a generic website link; the former is a narrower, more specific use of the latter.
findings.qaRationale: gs1:referencedFileURL is a generic web-resource/file link (labeled "Website") whose meaning carries nothing about identity or verifiable credentials, so treating our identity-credential pointer as its narrower child is a meaningless pan-URL mapping rather than a genuine conceptual specialization.

findings.rationale: Both properties refer to a URL that points to a verifiable digital identity credential, but the upstream term is more specific to a Digital Identity Anchor.
findings.qaRationale: Our generic 'URL to an identity verifiable credential' overlaps the UNTP DigitalIdentityAnchor concept but is broader and less specific, so it is not an exact interchangeable match.

findings.rationale: Both represent an element within a collection, but the OEC dataElement is specifically a member of DigitalProductPassport structures, whereas schema.org’s dataFeedElement is a generic feed item.
findings.qaRationale: schema.org dataFeedElement specifically links a DataFeed to a syndication DataFeedItem, which is a different concept from a structural member DataElement of a DPP per EN 18223; the only overlap is the generic notion of membership-in-a-collection, which is too weak to constitute the same concept.

findings.rationale: Both properties refer to an energy‑efficiency attribute, but the candidate is a specific classification property while our term is a generic energy‑efficiency indicator.
findings.qaRationale: Both are energy-efficiency properties that overlap, but the candidate denotes a specific efficiency classification (e.g. A–G label) while our undefined term reads as a generic efficiency attribute, so they overlap without being interchangeable.

findings.rationale: The OEC energyEfficiency property is a general concept, while schema.org’s energyEfficiencyScaleMax specifies only the maximum class on a particular scale.
findings.qaRationale: schema.org energyEfficiencyScaleMax denotes the upper boundary class of a regulated rating scale (scale metadata), not a product's energy-efficiency value, so it is a different concept from our general oec:energyEfficiency rather than a narrower instance of it.

findings.rationale: The OEC energyEfficiency property is a general concept of energy efficiency, whereas hasEnergyEfficiencyCategory specifies a particular category or rating within an international standard.
findings.qaRationale: Our bare energyEfficiency property (range oec:EnergyEfficiency) denotes the specific efficiency category/rating of a product, which is the same concept schema.org expresses as hasEnergyEfficiencyCategory, so ours is at most equally-or-more specific rather than broader.

findings.rationale: Both properties refer to the energy efficiency category of a product, but our term is generic while the candidate specifies it as part of consumption details.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties expressing a product's energy efficiency class/rating, but ours points directly to an efficiency value while schema:hasEnergyConsumptionDetails links to a broader consumption-details object, so they overlap without being interchangeable.

findings.rationale: EPREL Registration Number is a specific type of register entry, whereas registryEntry refers to any generic register entry.
findings.qaRationale: Both concern register entries so they overlap, but gs1:registryEntry models a link to a register resource (oriented to business/location alternative identifiers) while ours is a literal xsd:string for a product energy-labelling registry, so the type and scope differ enough to reject a clean NARROW subsumption.

findings.rationale: Our EPREL Registration Number is a specific type of regulatory verification number used for EU energy labelling, whereas the candidate term is a general regulatory verification number.
findings.qaRationale: GS1's regulatoryVerificationNumber is an access/verification credential used alongside a reference number to retrieve a regulatory document, whereas EPREL registration number is the primary product-registration identifier in the EPREL registry, so the concepts differ in role rather than being broader/narrower.

findings.rationale: EPREL Registration Number is a specific type of regulatory reference number, whereas regulatoryReferenceNumber covers any record in any regulatory system.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties carrying a registry reference number, and the EPREL registration number is a specific instance of the generic regulatoryReferenceNumber, so ours is narrower.

findings.rationale: The candidate property refers to general sustainability and recycling information, whereas our term specifically links a product to its recyclability assessment.
findings.qaRationale: The candidate is a deprecated, general-purpose free-text sustainability-and-recycling info property, not a concept-equivalent link to a structured recyclability assessment, so no graded match should be asserted against a deprecated term.

findings.rationale: Both classes denote the level of product hierarchy at which attribute sets are reported in a Digital Product Passport.
findings.qaRationale: Both classes model DPP reporting granularity at a product-hierarchy level, but ours frames it as the granularity characteristic of an attribute set while UNTP's GranularityLevel is specifically the level enumeration (and lacks a definition to confirm 1:1 interchangeability), so they overlap rather than coincide exactly.

findings.rationale: DPPGranularity specifies the granularity of attributes within a Digital Product Passport, whereas ProductIDGranularity is a broader concept describing the granularity of product identifiers.
findings.qaRationale: Both are class-level enumerations of product-hierarchy granularity levels, but ours frames granularity as the level a DPP attribute set describes while UNTP frames it as the granularity of a product identifier, so they overlap without being interchangeable or in a clean hierarchy.

findings.rationale: Both properties are URLs for compliance verification, but the candidate refers to any certification agency while ours is specifically for EPR scheme operator verification.
findings.qaRationale: GS1's property points to the website of an organization issuing a certification standard (e.g. MSC/FSC), whereas ours points to a producer-specific EPR scheme operator's verification endpoint; these are distinct concepts, not a genuine broader/narrower pair.

findings.rationale: oec:expectedLifespan expects a gs1:QuantitativeValue (value plus unit), whereas the GS1 value property is a generic numeric measurement; thus our term is more specific.
findings.qaRationale: oec:expectedLifespan is a semantic property denoting product longevity whose range happens to be gs1:QuantitativeValue, whereas gs1:value is the generic structural numeric slot inside such a value object; they are different concepts, not a broad/narrow pair.

findings.rationale: Both terms denote the same concept of a lifecycle boundary for emissions or environmental impact calculations.
findings.qaRationale: Names and class type align and both sit in the emissions/LCA domain, but the upstream term has no definition to confirm it denotes the same lifecycle-boundary concept rather than, e.g., GHG-Protocol operational scopes, so identity cannot be asserted.

findings.rationale: Both properties represent the fraction of a product that can be recycled, expressed as a decimal between 0 and 1.
findings.qaRationale: The upstream property shares an identical name and is also a property, but with no upstream definition I cannot confirm the scale, domain, or that 'recyclable' (not 'recycled') content is meant identically, so EXACT is not warranted.

findings.rationale: Both properties represent the fraction of a product that can be recycled, expressed as a decimal between 0 and 1.
findings.qaRationale: UNTP recycledContent measures the recycled material already in the product (post-input), whereas our recyclableContent measures the fraction that can be recycled at end-of-life; these are distinct circularity metrics, so the first-pass EXACT is wrong and at most a partial/narrower overlap exists, tending toward NONE.
